narrative:
"Such is the good and true City or State, and the good and true man is of the same pattern; and if this is right every other is wrong; and the evil is one which affects not only the ordering of the State, but also the regulation of the individual soul, and is exhibited in four forms."

"What are they?" he said.

I was proceeding to tell the order in which the four evil forms appeared to me to succeed one another, when Polemarchus, who was sitting a little way off, just beyond Adeimantus, began to whisper to him: stretching forth his hand, he took hold of the upper part of his coat by the shoulder, and drew him towards him, leaning forward himself so as to be quite close and saying something in his ear, of which I only caught the words, "Shall we let him off, or what shall we do?"
refashioned:
"So that's the good and true City or State, and a good and true person follows the same pattern. If this model is right, every other is wrong -- and the corruption that ruins a State also ruins the individual soul, manifesting in four distinct forms."

"What are they?" Glaucon asked.

I was about to explain the order in which these four corrupt forms succeed one another, when Polemarchus, sitting a short distance away just past Adeimantus, began whispering to him. He reached out, grabbed the shoulder of Adeimantus's cloak, and pulled him close, leaning in to murmur something in his ear. I caught only the words: "Should we let him off, or what should we do?"
elegant: Socrates declares that the good State and the good individual share the same pattern -- all deviations from this are corrupt, manifesting in four forms. Before he can enumerate them, Polemarchus whispers to Adeimantus, conspiring to press Socrates on a point he has skipped over.
summary: As Socrates prepares to describe four forms of corrupt government, Polemarchus secretly confers with Adeimantus about whether to let Socrates skip over an important topic he has glossed over.
distilled: Socrates is interrupted mid-argument by his companions conspiring to force him back to an unfinished topic.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (narrator), with Polemarchus and Adeimantus conspiring -- Polemarchus is the son of Cephalus, in whose house the Republic is set (Book I) -- The 'four evil forms' refer to timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny, discussed in Books VIII-IX -- This interruption redirects the dialogue to Book V's topics: equality of women, community of families, and philosopher-kings

narrative:
"Certainly not," said Adeimantus, raising his voice.

"Who is it," I said, "whom you are refusing to let off?"

"You," he said.

I repeated, "Why am I especially not to be let off?"

"Why," he said, "we think that you are lazy, and mean to cheat us out of a whole chapter which is a very important part of the story; and you fancy that we shall not notice your airy way of proceeding; as if it were self-evident to everybody, that in the matter of women and children 'friends have all things in common.'"
refashioned:
"Absolutely not," said Adeimantus, raising his voice.

"Who is it you're refusing to let off?" I asked.

"You," he said.

"Why me in particular?" I pressed.

"Because we think you're being lazy," he replied. "You're trying to cheat us out of an entire chapter that's a crucial part of the argument. You assume we won't notice your breezy way of skipping over it -- as though it were obvious to everyone that when it comes to women and children, 'friends share all things in common.'"
elegant: Adeimantus accuses Socrates of lazily skipping a crucial topic -- the meaning of 'friends have all things in common' as applied to women and children among the guardians.
summary: Adeimantus confronts Socrates, accusing him of trying to skip past the important question of what it means for the guardians to share women and children in common, treating it as self-evident when it requires thorough explanation.
distilled: Adeimantus demands Socrates explain what communal sharing of women and children actually means rather than glossing over it.
notes: Speakers: Adeimantus and Socrates -- 'Friends have all things in common' is a Pythagorean proverb, attributed in various forms throughout Greek philosophy -- Adeimantus, brother of Glaucon and Plato, serves as a persistent questioner throughout the Republic -- The 'whole chapter' refers to what becomes Book V's central content

narrative:
"And was I not right, Adeimantus?"

"Yes," he said; "but what is right in this particular case, like everything else, requires to be explained; for community may be of many kinds. Please, therefore, to say what sort of community you mean. We have been long expecting that you would tell us something about the family life of your citizens -- how they will bring children into the world, and rear them when they have arrived, and, in general, what is the nature of this community of women and children -- for we are of opinion that the right or wrong management of such matters will have a great and paramount influence on the State for good or for evil. And now, since the question is still undetermined, and you are taking in hand another State, we have resolved, as you heard, not to let you go until you give an account of all this."

"To that resolution," said Glaucon, "you may regard me as saying 'Agreed.'"
refashioned:
"Wasn't I right about that, Adeimantus?"

"Yes," he said, "but being right in principle, like everything else, still requires explanation. 'Community' can mean many things. So please specify what kind of community you have in mind. We've been waiting for you to address the family life of your citizens -- how they'll have children, how they'll raise them, and what this sharing of women and children really looks like in practice. We believe that getting this right or wrong will have an enormous impact on the State, for better or worse. Since you've left this question unanswered and are already moving on to the next topic, we've resolved -- as you overheard -- not to let you proceed until you give a full account."

"Count me in on that resolution," said Glaucon.
elegant: Adeimantus insists that 'community' can take many forms and demands Socrates specify how guardians will bear and raise children, since family arrangements will decisively shape the State. Glaucon seconds the demand.
summary: Adeimantus presses Socrates to explain precisely what communal family life means for the guardians -- how children will be born and raised -- arguing that these arrangements will have paramount influence on the State's success or failure. Glaucon agrees.
distilled: The interlocutors demand a detailed account of how communal child-rearing will work, insisting it is crucial to the State's welfare.
notes: Speakers: Socrates, Adeimantus, and Glaucon -- This passage sets up the three 'waves' of Book V: (1) equality of women, (2) community of families, (3) philosopher-kings -- Glaucon's agreement signals unanimous pressure on Socrates -- The concern about 'right or wrong management' anticipates the eugenics program Socrates will propose

narrative:
"And without more ado," said Thrasymachus, "you may consider us all to be equally agreed."

I said, "You know not what you are doing in thus assailing me: What an argument are you raising about the State! Just as I thought that I had finished, and was only too glad that I had laid this question to sleep, and was reflecting how fortunate I was in your acceptance of what I then said, you ask me to begin again at the very foundation, ignorant of what a hornet's nest of words you are stirring. Now I foresaw this gathering trouble, and avoided it."

"For what purpose do you conceive that we have come here," said Thrasymachus, "-- to look for gold, or to hear discourse?"
refashioned:
"You can consider us all unanimously agreed," said Thrasymachus without hesitation.

"You have no idea what you're doing by pressing me on this," I said. "What an argument you're opening up about the State! Just when I thought I was finished -- grateful that I'd put this question to rest and relieved that you'd accepted what I said earlier -- you're asking me to start over from the foundations. You don't realize what a hornet's nest of controversy you're stirring up. I saw this trouble coming, which is exactly why I avoided it."

"What do you think we came here for?" said Thrasymachus. "To look for gold, or to hear philosophical discussion?"
elegant: Thrasymachus confirms universal agreement that Socrates must address the topic. Socrates protests that they are reopening a hornet's nest he deliberately avoided. Thrasymachus retorts that they came for discourse, not gold.
summary: When all interlocutors insist Socrates address communal families, he protests that they are stirring up a hornet's nest of controversy he intentionally sidestepped. Thrasymachus reminds him that philosophical discourse is exactly what they came for.
distilled: Socrates warns his companions they are opening a hornet's nest, but Thrasymachus insists discourse is their purpose.
notes: Speakers: Thrasymachus and Socrates -- Thrasymachus, the sophist from Chalcedon, was Socrates' main antagonist in Book I -- His reappearance here is notable; he has been largely silent since his defeat in Book I -- 'To look for gold, or to hear discourse' echoes the Greek philosophical tradition of valuing logos over material wealth -- The 'hornet's nest' metaphor anticipates the three controversial 'waves' Socrates must face

narrative:
"Yes, but discourse should have a limit."

"Yes, Socrates," said Glaucon, "and the whole of life is the only limit which wise men assign to the hearing of such discourses. But never mind about us; take heart yourself and answer the question in your own way: What sort of community of women and children is this which is to prevail among our guardians? and how shall we manage the period between birth and education, which seems to require the greatest care? Tell us how these things will be."
refashioned:
"Yes, but there should be limits to discussion."

"Socrates," said Glaucon, "the only limit wise people set on hearing such discussions is the span of a lifetime. Don't worry about us -- gather your courage and answer the question however you see fit. What kind of community of women and children will exist among our guardians? And how should we handle the period between birth and education, which seems to demand the greatest care? Tell us how all this will work."
elegant: Glaucon declares that a lifetime is the only proper limit for philosophical discourse and urges Socrates to explain the guardians' communal arrangements for women, children, and early education.
summary: When Socrates suggests discourse should have limits, Glaucon counters that wise men devote their whole lives to such discussions and presses him to explain the guardians' system of shared families and child-rearing.
distilled: Glaucon insists philosophy deserves a lifetime's attention and urges Socrates to detail the guardians' communal family system.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's enthusiasm for unlimited philosophical inquiry contrasts with Socrates' reluctance -- The 'period between birth and education' anticipates the state-managed nursery system Socrates will describe -- This echoes Socrates' famous claim that 'the unexamined life is not worth living' (Apology 38a)

narrative:
"Yes, my simple friend, but the answer is the reverse of easy; many more doubts arise about this than about our previous conclusions. For the practicability of what is said may be doubted; and looked at in another point of view, whether the scheme, if ever so practicable, would be for the best, is also doubtful. Hence I feel a reluctance to approach the subject, lest our aspiration, my dear friend, should turn out to be a dream only."

"Fear not," he replied, "for your audience will not be hard upon you; they are not sceptical or hostile."

I said: "My good friend, I suppose that you mean to encourage me by these words."
refashioned:
"My friend, the answer is far from easy. This raises many more doubts than our previous conclusions. First, the practicability of the proposal can be questioned. Second, even if it were entirely practicable, whether it would actually be for the best is also uncertain. So I'm reluctant to approach the subject, for fear that our aspiration may turn out to be nothing more than a dream."

"Don't be afraid," Glaucon replied. "Your audience won't be hard on you. They're neither skeptical nor hostile."

"I suppose you mean to encourage me by saying that," I said.
elegant: Socrates expresses reluctance -- the proposal raises doubts about both practicability and desirability, and may prove to be mere dreaming. Glaucon assures him the audience is sympathetic.
summary: Socrates hesitates to address the communal family system, fearing it may be impractical or undesirable -- a mere dream. Glaucon reassures him that his audience is friendly and open-minded.
distilled: Socrates fears his proposals about communal families may be impractical dreams, but Glaucon assures a sympathetic hearing.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates' hesitation is partly dramatic and partly genuine philosophical caution -- The distinction between practicability and desirability becomes a recurring methodological theme in Book V -- 'Dream' versus reality anticipates the later epistemological discussion about opinion versus knowledge (5.80-5.88)

narrative:
"Yes," he said.

"Then let me tell you that you are doing just the reverse; the encouragement which you offer would have been all very well had I myself believed that I knew what I was talking about: to declare the truth about matters of high interest which a man honours and loves among wise men who love him need occasion no fear or faltering in his mind; but to carry on an argument when you are yourself only a hesitating enquirer, which is my condition, is a dangerous and slippery thing; and the danger is not that I shall be laughed at (of which the fear would be childish), but that I shall miss the truth where I have most need to be sure of my footing, and drag my friends after me in my fall. And I pray Nemesis not to visit upon me the words which I am going to utter. For I do indeed believe that to be an involuntary homicide is a less crime than to be a deceiver about beauty or goodness or justice in the matter of laws. And that is a risk which I would rather run among enemies than among friends, and therefore you do well to encourage me."
refashioned:
"Yes," he said.

"Then you're actually doing the opposite of encouraging me," I replied. "Your reassurance would be fine if I were confident I knew what I was talking about. A man who speaks truth on matters he honors and loves, among wise friends who love him, has nothing to fear. But arguing when you're only a hesitant inquirer yourself -- which is my position -- is dangerous and treacherous ground. The danger isn't that I'll be laughed at; that fear would be childish. The danger is that I'll miss the truth precisely where I need it most, and drag my friends down with me in the fall.

I pray that Nemesis won't punish me for what I'm about to say. I genuinely believe that involuntary manslaughter is a lesser crime than deceiving people about beauty, goodness, or justice when it comes to laws. And that's a risk I'd rather take among enemies than among friends -- so in that sense, yes, your encouragement is well-placed."
elegant: Socrates argues that friendly encouragement actually increases the stakes: his real fear is not ridicule but misleading his friends on matters of justice and goodness. He considers intellectual deception worse than involuntary homicide and invokes Nemesis against his own potential errors.
summary: Socrates explains that the danger of philosophical inquiry among friends is not mockery but leading them astray on questions of justice and law. He considers deceiving others about goodness to be a worse crime than involuntary killing.
distilled: Socrates fears misleading his friends about justice more than he fears ridicule, considering intellectual deception worse than involuntary manslaughter.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Nemesis is the Greek goddess of retribution who punishes hubris -- The distinction between involuntary homicide and deliberate deception echoes the Socratic position that wrongdoing stems from ignorance (Protagoras, Meno) -- 'Hesitating enquirer' reflects Socratic irony and the method of aporia -- The passage foreshadows Socrates' controversial claims about philosopher-kings

narrative:
Glaucon laughed and said: "Well then, Socrates, in case you and your argument do us any serious injury you shall be acquitted beforehand of the homicide and shall not be held to be a deceiver; take courage then and speak."

"Well," I said, "the law says that when a man is acquitted he is free from guilt, and what holds at law may hold in argument."

"Then why should you mind?"

"Well," I replied, "I suppose that I must retrace my steps and say what I perhaps ought to have said before in the proper place. The part of the men has been played out, and now properly enough comes the turn of the women. Of them I will proceed to speak, and the more readily since I am invited by you.

For men born and educated like our citizens, the only way, in my opinion, of arriving at a right conclusion about the possession and use of women and children is to follow the path on which we originally started, when we said that the men were to be the guardians and watchdogs of the herd."

"True."

"Let us further suppose the birth and education of our women to be subject to similar or nearly similar regulations; then we shall see whether the result accords with our design."
refashioned:
Glaucon laughed. "Well then, Socrates, if you and your argument do us any serious harm, consider yourself acquitted of the homicide in advance. You won't be held a deceiver. Take courage and speak."

"The law does say that an acquitted man is free from guilt," I said, "and what holds in law may hold in argument."

"Then what's stopping you?"

"Very well," I replied. "I suppose I must go back and address what I should have covered earlier, in its proper place. We've dealt with the men's role; now it's the women's turn. And I'll take this up all the more readily since you're urging me to do so.

For people born and educated as our citizens are, the only sound approach to the question of women and children is to follow the path we originally set out on -- when we said that the men were to be guardians and watchdogs of the community."

"True."

"Then let us suppose that the birth and education of women should follow the same or similar regulations as the men's. We'll see whether the result fits our design."
elegant: Glaucon playfully acquits Socrates in advance. Socrates agrees to retrace his steps: since the men are guardians and watchdogs of the State, the question of women must follow the same foundational logic. He proposes giving women the same birth conditions and education as men, to test whether the results accord with the State's design.
summary: After Glaucon grants him advance acquittal for any intellectual harm, Socrates agrees to address the role of women. He argues that since men serve as guardians, the only coherent approach is to apply the same or similar educational regulations to women and see if the results fit the State's design.
distilled: Socrates proposes that women should receive the same education and training as male guardians to maintain consistency in the State's design.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- The 'guardians and watchdogs' metaphor was established in Books II-III -- 'The part of the men has been played out' refers to the guardian class system developed in Books II-IV -- This begins the first 'wave' of Book V: the equality of women -- The legal metaphor of acquittal plays on the earlier comparison of deception to homicide

narrative:
"What do you mean?"

"What I mean may be put into the form of a question," I said: "Are dogs divided into hes and shes, or do they both share equally in hunting and in keeping watch and in the other duties of dogs? or do we entrust to the males the entire and exclusive care of the flocks, while we leave the females at home, under the idea that the bearing and suckling their puppies is labour enough for them?"
refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"Let me put it as a question," I said. "With dogs, do we divide duties by sex, or do males and females share equally in hunting, guarding, and every other task? Or do we assign all the work of protecting the flocks to the males alone, while keeping the females at home on the assumption that bearing and nursing puppies is labor enough for them?"
elegant: Socrates poses the analogy: do we divide dogs' duties by sex, or do males and females share in hunting and guarding alike? Or do we confine females to breeding while males do all the work?
summary: Socrates introduces his argument for women's equality through an analogy with dogs: do we separate canine duties by sex, or do both male and female dogs share in hunting and guarding?
distilled: Socrates uses the dog analogy to ask whether duties should be divided by sex or shared equally.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- The dog/guardian analogy was established in Book II (375a-376c) where Socrates compared ideal guardians to well-bred watchdogs -- This is a classic Socratic analogical argument: if female dogs can guard and hunt, so can female guardians -- The analogy deliberately challenges Athenian norms where women were confined to the household (oikos)

narrative:
"No," he said, "they share alike; the only difference between them is that the males are stronger and the females weaker."

"But can you use different animals for the same purpose, unless they are bred and fed in the same way?"

"You cannot."

"Then, if women are to have the same duties as men, they must have the same nurture and education?"

"Yes."

"The education which was assigned to the men was music and gymnastic."

"Yes."

"Then women must be taught music and gymnastic and also the art of war, which they must practise like the men?"

"That is the inference, I suppose."

"I should rather expect," I said, "that several of our proposals, if they are carried out, being unusual, may appear ridiculous."

"No doubt of it."

"Yes, and the most ridiculous thing of all will be the sight of women naked in the palaestra, exercising with the men, especially when they are no longer young; they certainly will not be a vision of beauty, any more than the enthusiastic old men who in spite of wrinkles and ugliness continue to frequent the gymnasia."

"Yes, indeed," he said: "according to present notions the proposal would be thought ridiculous."

"But then," I said, "as we have determined to speak our minds, we must not fear the jests of the wits which will be directed against this sort of innovation; how they will talk of women's attainments both in music and gymnastic, and above all about their wearing armour and riding upon horseback!"
refashioned:
"No," Glaucon said, "they share duties alike. The only difference is that males are stronger and females weaker."

"But can you use different animals for the same purpose unless they're bred and trained the same way?"

"You can't."

"Then if women are to have the same duties as men, they must receive the same upbringing and education?"

"Yes."

"And the education we assigned to men was music and gymnastics."

"Yes."

"Then women must be taught music, gymnastics, and the art of war, practicing alongside the men?"

"That seems to follow."

"I should warn you," I said, "that several of these proposals, being so unconventional, will appear ridiculous."

"No doubt."

"And the most ridiculous sight of all will be women exercising naked in the gymnasium alongside men -- especially the older women. They won't exactly be a vision of beauty, any more than those enthusiastic old men who keep showing up at the gym despite their wrinkles."

"Yes," he said, "by current standards that would seem absurd."

"But since we've committed to speaking honestly," I said, "we can't let ourselves be intimidated by the mockery this will invite -- jokes about women in music and athletics, and above all, about women wearing armor and riding horses!"
elegant: Since male and female dogs share duties and differ only in strength, the same logic applies to guardians: women must receive the same education in music, gymnastics, and warfare. Socrates acknowledges this will seem ridiculous -- especially women exercising naked in gymnasia -- but insists they must not be deterred by mockery.
summary: Socrates argues from the dog analogy that since equal duties require equal training, women guardians must study music, gymnastics, and warfare alongside men. He acknowledges the proposal will seem ridiculous by current standards but insists on following the argument wherever it leads.
distilled: Equal duties require equal education: women guardians must train in music, gymnastics, and war alongside men, regardless of how ridiculous it appears.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The palaestra was the Greek wrestling school where men exercised naked (gymnos, root of 'gymnastics') -- Greek athletics were performed nude; proposing this for women was genuinely shocking -- Music (mousike) in Greek education included literature, poetry, and intellectual training, not just musical performance -- The education of guardians in music and gymnastic was established in Books II-III

narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"Yet having begun we must go forward to the rough places of the law; at the same time begging of these gentlemen for once in their life to be serious. Not long ago, as we shall remind them, the Hellenes were of the opinion, which is still generally received among the barbarians, that the sight of a naked man was ridiculous and improper; and when first the Cretans and then the Lacedaemonians introduced the custom, the wits of that day might equally have ridiculed the innovation."
refashioned:
"Very true," he replied.

"Yet having begun, we must press forward through the rough terrain of this argument. At the same time, let us ask our critics to be serious for once in their lives. Not long ago, the Greeks themselves believed -- as most non-Greek peoples still do -- that the sight of a naked man was ridiculous and improper. When the Cretans and then the Spartans first introduced the practice of male nudity in athletics, the comedians of that era could just as easily have mocked the innovation."
elegant: Having begun, they must press forward despite ridicule. Socrates reminds his audience that Greek male nudity in athletics was itself once considered absurd -- the Cretans and Spartans introduced it against mockery, and custom eventually vindicated them.
summary: Socrates argues that what seems ridiculous now may prove wise later, pointing out that Greek male athletic nudity was itself once considered shameful and absurd before being adopted through Cretan and Spartan example.
distilled: What seems ridiculous in custom may prove wise in practice, as the once-mocked Greek adoption of male athletic nudity demonstrates.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The Cretans and Lacedaemonians (Spartans) were credited with introducing gymnastic nudity, probably in the 8th-7th century BCE -- Thucydides (1.6) also notes that athletic nudity was a relatively recent Greek innovation -- 'Barbarians' (barbaroi) meant non-Greek speakers, not necessarily uncivilized -- Spartan women notably did receive physical training, making Sparta a partial real-world precedent for Socrates' proposals

narrative: "No doubt. But when experience showed that to let all things be uncovered was far better than to cover them up, and the ludicrous effect to the outward eye vanished before the better principle which reason asserted, then the man was perceived to be a fool who directs the shafts of his ridicule at any other sight but that of folly and vice, or seriously inclines to weigh the beautiful by any other standard but that of the good."
refashioned: "No doubt. But when experience demonstrated that exercising uncovered was far better than exercising covered up, the initial absurdity faded as reason revealed the deeper principle. At that point, anyone who directed ridicule at anything other than folly and vice was himself revealed as a fool -- just as anyone who measured beauty by any standard other than the good was shown to be misguided."
elegant: When experience showed that uncovered exercise was superior, reason triumphed over mockery. The true fool is one who ridicules anything other than vice, or who measures beauty by any standard other than the good.
summary: Experience vindicated the practice of athletic nudity, proving that reason outweighs initial ridicule. The real fool is one who mocks anything other than genuine vice, or who judges beauty by any standard other than goodness.
distilled: Reason and experience outweigh mockery: true ridicule should target only vice, and true beauty is measured only by the good.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This establishes a key Platonic aesthetic principle: the beautiful (to kalon) is identified with the good (to agathon) -- This principle recurs throughout the Republic and is central to Books VI-VII -- The argument that custom evolves through reason anticipates the progressive logic of the entire Book V

narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"First, then, whether the question is to be put in jest or in earnest, let us come to an understanding about the nature of woman: Is she capable of sharing either wholly or partially in the actions of men, or not at all? And is the art of war one of those arts in which she can or can not share? That will be the best way of commencing the enquiry, and will probably lead to the fairest conclusion."

"That will be much the best way."

"Shall we take the other side first and begin by arguing against ourselves; in this manner the adversary's position will not be undefended."
refashioned:
"Very true," he replied.

"First, then -- whether we treat this seriously or playfully -- let us reach an understanding about the nature of women. Are they capable of sharing in men's activities fully, partially, or not at all? And specifically, is warfare one of those activities they can or cannot share in? This will be the best starting point and will likely lead to the fairest conclusion."

"That's by far the best approach."

"Should we begin by arguing against our own position? That way, the opposing case won't go unrepresented."
elegant: Socrates proposes to investigate whether women can share in men's activities fully, partially, or not at all -- especially warfare. He suggests they first argue against their own position so the opposing view is fairly represented.
summary: Socrates frames the central question: can women share in men's activities, including warfare? He proposes a fair inquiry by first arguing against their own position, ensuring the opposition is properly represented.
distilled: The inquiry begins: can women share in men's work and warfare? Socrates proposes to steelman the opposing case first.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Socrates' method of arguing against himself anticipates modern steelmanning and dialectical method -- The question of women's capacity for warfare was radical in Athens, where women had no political or military role -- Sparta, by contrast, did train women physically, though not for direct combat -- This methodological fairness is characteristic of Socratic dialectic

narrative:
"Why not?" he said.

"Then let us put a speech into the mouths of our opponents. They will say: 'Socrates and Glaucon, no adversary need convict you, for you yourselves, at the first foundation of the State, admitted the principle that everybody was to do the one work suited to his own nature.' And certainly, if I am not mistaken, such an admission was made by us. 'And do not the natures of men and women differ very much indeed?' And we shall reply: Of course they do. 'Then we shall be asked, Whether the tasks assigned to men and to women should not be different, and such as are agreeable to their different natures?' Certainly they should. 'But if so, have you not fallen into a serious inconsistency in saying that men and women, whose natures are so entirely different, ought to perform the same actions?' -- What defence will you make for us, my good Sir, against any one who offers these objections?"
refashioned:
"Why not?" he said.

"Then let us give voice to our opponents' argument. They would say: 'Socrates and Glaucon, you don't even need an adversary to refute you -- you've done it yourselves. When you first founded the State, you established the principle that everyone should do the one kind of work suited to their own nature.' And indeed, if I'm not mistaken, we did admit that. 'And don't the natures of men and women differ significantly?' We'd have to agree they do. 'Then shouldn't the tasks assigned to men and women be different, matching their different natures?' Of course. 'But then haven't you fallen into a serious contradiction by saying that men and women -- whose natures are so different -- should perform the same work?'

What defense would you offer, Glaucon, against someone who raises these objections?"
elegant: Socrates voices the strongest objection: they themselves established that each person should do work suited to their nature. Since men's and women's natures differ, assigning them the same work appears self-contradictory. He challenges Glaucon to find a defense.
summary: Socrates constructs the opponent's argument: since the State's founding principle is that everyone should do work suited to their nature, and men and women have different natures, isn't it contradictory to assign them the same tasks?
distilled: The apparent contradiction: if different natures should have different work, and men and women differ in nature, how can they share the same duties?
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The principle of specialization ('one person, one work') was established in Book II (369e-370c) as the foundation of the just city -- This is a genuine logical challenge that Socrates must resolve, not merely a straw man -- The resolution will depend on distinguishing relevant from irrelevant natural differences -- This dialectical move exemplifies the elenchus method

narrative:
"That is not an easy question to answer when asked suddenly; and I shall and I do beg of you to draw out the case on our side."

"These are the objections, Glaucon, and there are many others of a like kind, which I foresaw long ago; they made me afraid and reluctant to take in hand any law about the possession and nurture of women and children."

"By Zeus," he said, "the problem to be solved is anything but easy."

"Why yes," I said, "but the fact is that when a man is out of his depth, whether he has fallen into a little swimming bath or into mid-ocean, he has to swim all the same."

"Very true."

"And must not we swim and try to reach the shore: we will hope that Arion's dolphin or some other miraculous help may save us?"

"I suppose so," he said.

"Well then, let us see if any way of escape can be found. We acknowledged -- did we not? -- that different natures ought to have different pursuits, and that men's and women's natures are different. And now what are we saying? -- that different natures ought to have the same pursuits -- this is the inconsistency which is charged upon us."

"Precisely."

"Verily, Glaucon," I said, "glorious is the power of the art of contradiction!"

"Why do you say so?"

"Because I think that many a man falls into the practice against his will. When he thinks that he is reasoning he is really disputing, just because he cannot define and divide, and so know that of which he is speaking; and he will pursue a merely verbal opposition in the spirit of contention and not of fair discussion."

"Yes," he replied, "such is very often the case; but what has that to do with us and our argument?"

"A great deal; for there is certainly a danger of our getting unintentionally into a verbal opposition."

"In what way?"

"Why, we valiantly and pugnaciously insist upon the verbal truth, that different natures ought to have different pursuits, but we never considered at all what was the meaning of sameness or difference of nature, or why we distinguished them when we assigned different pursuits to different natures and the same to the same natures."
refashioned:
"That's not easy to answer on the spot. I beg you to make the case on our behalf."

"These objections, Glaucon, along with many others like them, are exactly what I foresaw long ago. They're what made me afraid and reluctant to propose any law about women and children."

"By Zeus," he said, "this is no easy problem."

"True," I said, "but when a man is out of his depth, whether he's fallen into a kiddie pool or the middle of the ocean, he has to swim regardless."

"Very true."

"So we must swim for shore. Perhaps Arion's dolphin or some other miraculous rescue will save us."

"I suppose so."

"Well then, let's look for a way through. We agreed that different natures should have different pursuits, and that men's and women's natures are different. Now we're saying that different natures should have the same pursuits. That's the contradiction we're charged with."

"Precisely."

"How powerful the art of contradiction is, Glaucon!"

"What do you mean?"

"Many people fall into mere contradiction without realizing it. They think they're reasoning when they're actually just arguing -- because they can't properly define and distinguish their terms. They pursue verbal disputes in a spirit of contention rather than genuine inquiry."

"Yes, that happens constantly. But what does it have to do with our argument?"

"Everything. There's a real danger that we've slipped into a merely verbal opposition ourselves."

"How so?"

"We stubbornly insisted on the principle that different natures should have different pursuits, but we never actually examined what we meant by 'sameness' or 'difference' of nature. We never asked which differences matter when assigning different pursuits to different natures."
elegant: Glaucon is stumped. Socrates notes they must swim regardless of depth, then identifies the key error: they have been engaging in verbal contradiction without examining what 'difference in nature' actually means. The crucial unasked question is which differences in nature are relevant to assigning different pursuits.
summary: Socrates identifies the root of the apparent contradiction: they insisted that different natures require different work but never defined what counts as a relevant 'difference in nature.' Many people fall into mere verbal disputes because they fail to define their terms precisely. The real question is which natural differences actually matter for assigning different roles.
distilled: The apparent contradiction dissolves when we ask: which differences in nature are actually relevant to assigning different work?
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Arion was a legendary poet saved from drowning by a dolphin, as told by Herodotus (1.23-24) -- The distinction between eristic (contentious arguing) and dialectic (genuine inquiry) is central to Platonic philosophy, explored extensively in Euthydemus and Sophist -- The key philosophical move here -- distinguishing relevant from irrelevant differences -- is a foundational principle of logic and classification -- 'Define and divide' (diairesis) is the Platonic method of classification, developed further in Phaedrus and Sophist

narrative:
"Why, no," he said, "that was never considered by us."

I said: "Suppose that by way of illustration we were to ask the question whether there is not an opposition in nature between bald men and hairy men; and if this is admitted by us, then, if bald men are cobblers, we should forbid the hairy men to be cobblers, and conversely?"
refashioned:
"No," he said, "we never considered that."

"Suppose, as an illustration," I said, "we asked whether there's a natural opposition between bald men and hairy men. If we admitted that there was, then -- following our principle -- if bald men are cobblers, we'd have to forbid hairy men from being cobblers, and vice versa."
elegant: Socrates uses a reductio ad absurdum: if any natural difference justified different roles, then bald men and hairy men would have to practice different trades -- an obviously absurd conclusion.
summary: Socrates illustrates the absurdity of treating every natural difference as relevant by asking whether bald men and hairy men should be forbidden from practicing the same trade, since they differ in nature.
distilled: The bald-versus-hairy example shows that not every natural difference is relevant to role assignment.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is a classic reductio ad absurdum, a favorite Socratic technique -- The example is deliberately trivial to expose the logical error of treating all differences as relevant -- This move is crucial to the overall argument: biological sex differences (like baldness versus hairiness) may be similarly irrelevant to the capacity for guardianship

narrative:
"That would be a jest," he said.

"Yes," I said, "a jest; and why? because we never meant when we constructed the State, that the opposition of natures should extend to every difference, but only to those differences which affected the pursuit in which the individual is engaged; we should have argued, for example, that a physician and one who is in mind a physician may be said to have the same nature."

"True."

"Whereas the physician and the carpenter have different natures?"

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"That would be absurd," he said.

"Exactly -- absurd. And why? Because when we founded the State, we never intended the principle of 'different natures, different work' to apply to every conceivable difference between people. We meant it to apply only to differences relevant to the pursuit in question. For example, we'd say that a physician and someone with a physician's aptitude share the same nature."

"True."

"While a physician and a carpenter have genuinely different natures?"

"Certainly."
elegant: The principle of specialization was never meant to apply to every difference -- only to differences relevant to the work in question. A physician and a natural-born healer share the same nature; a physician and a carpenter do not.
summary: Socrates clarifies that the principle of different natures requiring different work applies only to differences relevant to the specific pursuit -- not to all differences. A physician and someone with healing aptitude share the same nature; a physician and carpenter do not.
distilled: Only differences relevant to a specific pursuit matter for role assignment, not all natural differences.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This distinction between relevant and irrelevant differences is the philosophical key to the entire argument for women's equality -- The physician/carpenter example shows that 'nature' in this context means aptitude for a particular activity -- This anticipates modern arguments about the difference between sex (biological) and relevant occupational capacity

narrative: "And if," I said, "the male and female sex appear to differ in their fitness for any art or pursuit, we should say that such pursuit or art ought to be assigned to one or the other of them; but if the difference consists only in women bearing and men begetting children, this does not amount to a proof that a woman differs from a man in respect of the sort of education she should receive; and we shall therefore continue to maintain that our guardians and their wives ought to have the same pursuits."
refashioned: "If men and women prove to differ in their aptitude for a specific art or pursuit," I said, "then we should assign that pursuit to one sex or the other. But if the only difference between them is that women bear children and men beget them, that alone doesn't prove women differ from men in the kind of education they should receive. We should therefore continue to maintain that our guardians and their wives ought to share the same pursuits."
elegant: If men and women differ in aptitude for a specific pursuit, that pursuit should be assigned accordingly. But the mere biological difference of childbearing versus begetting does not prove women differ from men in their capacity for education or guardianship.
summary: Socrates argues that the only proven difference between men and women -- that women bear children and men beget them -- is not relevant to their capacity for education or guardianship. Unless a specific aptitude difference can be shown, women should share men's pursuits.
distilled: The biological difference of childbearing does not prove women are unfit for the same education and pursuits as men.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This is the crux of Socrates' argument for women's equality: reproductive difference does not entail difference in intellectual or martial capacity -- This was a remarkably progressive position for 4th-century BCE Athens -- The argument anticipates modern feminist philosophy's distinction between sex and gender roles -- Compare Aristophanes' Assemblywomen (Ecclesiazusae), a comedy about women taking over government, performed around the same period

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Next, we shall ask our opponent how, in reference to any of the pursuits or arts of civic life, the nature of a woman differs from that of a man?"

"That will be quite fair."

"And perhaps he, like yourself, will reply that to give a sufficient answer on the instant is not easy; but after a little reflection there is no difficulty."

"Yes, perhaps."

"Suppose then that we invite him to accompany us in the argument, and then we may hope to show him that there is nothing peculiar in the constitution of women which would affect them in the administration of the State."
refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"Next, let's challenge our opponent: in what specific civic art or pursuit does a woman's nature actually differ from a man's?"

"That's a fair question."

"And perhaps, like you, he'll say it's not easy to answer on the spot -- but with a little thought, it shouldn't be difficult."

"Perhaps."

"Then let's invite him to follow the argument with us. We may be able to show him that there is nothing in women's constitution that disqualifies them from participating in the administration of the State."
elegant: Socrates challenges opponents to identify any specific civic pursuit where women's nature differs from men's, confident the argument will show nothing in women's constitution disqualifies them from governing.
summary: Socrates challenges the opponent to name any specific civic art where women's nature genuinely differs from men's, confident that careful examination will reveal nothing in women's constitution that bars them from governing.
distilled: Socrates challenges opponents to identify any civic pursuit where women's nature actually differs from men's in a relevant way.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The burden of proof is shifted to the opponent: they must identify a specific relevant difference, not just assert general inferiority -- 'Administration of the State' (politeia) is the same word as the dialogue's Greek title -- This methodological move -- demanding specific evidence rather than accepting general assumptions -- is characteristically Socratic

narrative:
"By all means."

"Let us say to him: Come now, and we will ask you a question: -- when you spoke of a nature gifted or not gifted in any respect, did you mean to say that one man will acquire a thing easily, another with difficulty; a little learning will lead the one to discover a great deal; whereas the other, after much study and application, no sooner learns than he forgets; or again, did you mean, that the one has a body which is a good servant to his mind, while the body of the other is a hindrance to him? -- would not these be the sort of differences which distinguish the man gifted by nature from the one who is ungifted?"
refashioned:
"By all means."

"Let us ask him this: When you talk about someone being naturally gifted or ungifted for something, what do you mean? Do you mean that one person picks things up easily while another struggles; that a little learning leads one to great discoveries while the other forgets everything despite long study? Or do you mean that one person's body serves their mind well while the other's body holds them back? Aren't these the kinds of differences that distinguish natural talent from its absence?"
elegant: Socrates defines natural aptitude: ease of learning, retention of knowledge, and having a body that serves rather than hinders the mind. These are the relevant criteria for 'nature,' not sex.
summary: Socrates defines what it means to be naturally gifted: learning easily, retaining knowledge, and having a body that supports rather than hinders mental activity. These are the relevant criteria for determining who is suited for a pursuit.
distilled: Natural aptitude means ease of learning, good retention, and a body that serves the mind -- not biological sex.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This definition of natural aptitude focuses on cognitive and practical criteria, deliberately excluding sex as a category -- The body-mind relationship here anticipates later discussions in Book VII about the soul's education -- 'A body which is a good servant to his mind' reflects the Greek ideal of physical-intellectual harmony (kalokagathia)

narrative:
"No one will deny that."

"And can you mention any pursuit of mankind in which the male sex has not all these gifts and qualities in a higher degree than the female? Need I waste time in speaking of the art of weaving, and the management of pancakes and preserves, in which womankind does really appear to be great, and in which for her to be beaten by a man is of all things the most absurd?"
refashioned:
"No one would deny that."

"And can you name any human pursuit in which men don't generally surpass women in these aptitudes? I won't waste time discussing weaving or cooking, where women do genuinely excel and where it would be absurd for a man to outperform them."
elegant: Socrates concedes that men generally surpass women in degree of aptitude across most pursuits, while acknowledging women's superiority in domestic arts like weaving and cooking -- though he treats these as trivial.
summary: Socrates concedes that men generally exceed women in aptitude across most pursuits, though he acknowledges women's superiority in domestic arts like weaving and cooking, dismissing these as unworthy of extended discussion.
distilled: Men generally surpass women in degree of aptitude, though women excel in domestic arts -- but this difference of degree does not justify exclusion.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The concession about male superiority in degree is important: Socrates argues for equal opportunity despite unequal average ability -- 'Pancakes and preserves' reflects the Greek domestic sphere assigned to women -- The humor here is intentional -- Socrates is both acknowledging and gently mocking gendered assumptions -- This nuanced position distinguishes Socrates from both modern egalitarianism and ancient patriarchy

narrative:
"You are quite right," he replied, "in maintaining the general inferiority of the female sex: although many women are in many things superior to many men, yet on the whole what you say is true."

"And if so, my friend," I said, "there is no special faculty of administration in a state which a woman has because she is a woman, or which a man has by virtue of his sex, but the gifts of nature are alike diffused in both; all the pursuits of men are the pursuits of women also, but in all of them a woman is inferior to a man."

"Very true."

"Then are we to impose all our enactments on men and none of them on women?"

"That will never do."

"One woman has a gift of healing, another not; one is a musician, and another has no music in her nature?"

"Very true."

"And one woman has a turn for gymnastic and military exercises, and another is unwarlike and hates gymnastics?"

"Certainly."

"And one woman is a philosopher, and another is an enemy of philosophy; one has spirit, and another is without spirit?"

"That is also true."

"Then one woman will have the temper of a guardian, and another not. Was not the selection of the male guardians determined by differences of this sort?"

"Yes."

"Men and women alike possess the qualities which make a guardian; they differ only in their comparative strength or weakness."

"Obviously."

"And those women who have such qualities are to be selected as the companions and colleagues of men who have similar qualities and whom they resemble in capacity and in character?"

"Very true."

"And ought not the same natures to have the same pursuits?"

"They ought."

"Then, as we were saying before, there is nothing unnatural in assigning music and gymnastic to the wives of the guardians -- to that point we come round again."

"Certainly not."

"The law which we then enacted was agreeable to nature, and therefore not an impossibility or mere aspiration; and the contrary practice, which prevails at present, is in reality a violation of nature."
refashioned:
"You're right," Glaucon replied, "about the general inferiority of women in degree -- though many individual women are superior to many individual men in various things. On the whole, your point holds."

"Then there is no governing ability that a woman possesses because she is a woman, or that a man possesses by virtue of being a man," I said. "Natural gifts are distributed across both sexes. All of men's pursuits are also women's pursuits, though women are generally weaker in each."

"Very true."

"So should we impose all our laws on men and none on women?"

"Of course not."

"One woman has a gift for healing, another does not. One is musical, another has no music in her."

"True."

"One woman is inclined toward athletics and military training, while another is unwarlike and hates exercise."

"Certainly."

"One woman is a philosopher; another despises philosophy. One has spirit; another lacks it."

"Also true."

"So some women will have the temperament of a guardian, and others won't. Weren't the male guardians selected on exactly this basis?"

"Yes."

"Then men and women alike possess the qualities that make a guardian. They differ only in relative strength."

"Obviously."

"Women with these qualities should be selected as companions and colleagues of men with similar qualities, whom they resemble in capacity and character?"

"True."

"And shouldn't the same natures pursue the same activities?"

"They should."

"Then there is nothing unnatural about assigning music and gymnastics to the wives of guardians. We've come full circle on this point."

"Nothing unnatural at all."

"The law we enacted is in harmony with nature -- not an impossibility or wishful thinking. The current practice of excluding women is actually the violation of nature."
elegant: No governing ability belongs to one sex by nature. Natural gifts are distributed across both sexes; individuals of both sexes vary in aptitude for healing, music, athletics, philosophy, and spiritedness. Women with guardian temperaments should be selected as colleagues of similarly qualified men. The current exclusion of women, not their inclusion, violates nature.
summary: Socrates concludes that no political capacity belongs inherently to either sex. Natural talents are distributed across both, with individual variation within each sex. Women with guardian temperaments should serve alongside similarly qualified men. The current practice of excluding women from public life is the true violation of nature.
distilled: Natural gifts are distributed across both sexes; excluding qualified women from guardianship violates nature, while including them fulfills it.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the culmination of the first 'wave' argument -- The key distinction is between average group differences (women generally weaker) and individual variation (some women are stronger/wiser than some men) -- 'The contrary practice is a violation of nature' is a radical inversion of conventional Greek thinking -- Compare Aristotle's opposing view in Politics 1.13, where he argues women are naturally subordinate -- The phrase 'companions and colleagues' suggests genuine partnership, not subordination

narrative:
"We had to consider, first, whether our proposals were possible, and secondly whether they were the most beneficial?"

"Yes."

"And the possibility has been acknowledged?"

"Yes."

"The very great benefit has next to be established?"

"Quite so."

"You will admit that the same education which makes a man a good guardian will make a woman a good guardian; for their original nature is the same?"

"Yes."

"I should like to ask you a question."

"What is it?"

"Would you say that all men are equal in excellence, or is one man better than another?"

"The latter."

"And in the commonwealth which we were founding do you conceive the guardians who have been brought up on our model system to be more perfect men, or the cobblers whose education has been cobbling?"

"What a ridiculous question!"

"You have answered me," I replied: "Well, and may we not further say that our guardians are the best of our citizens?"

"By far the best."

"And will not their wives be the best women?"

"Yes, by far the best."

"And can there be anything better for the interests of the State than that the men and women of a State should be as good as possible?"

"There can be nothing better."

"And this is what the arts of music and gymnastic, when present in such manner as we have described, will accomplish?"

"Certainly."

"Then we have made an enactment not only possible but in the highest degree beneficial to the State?"
refashioned:
"We needed to establish two things: first, whether our proposals are possible, and second, whether they are maximally beneficial."

"Yes."

"We've established possibility. Now we must demonstrate the great benefit."

"Agreed."

"You'd admit that the same education that makes a man a good guardian will make a woman a good guardian, since their fundamental nature is the same?"

"Yes."

"Let me ask you something. Are all men equal in excellence, or are some better than others?"

"Some are better."

"In the State we're founding, are the guardians raised under our educational system more excellent, or the cobblers whose education has been cobbling?"

"What a ridiculous question!"

"You've answered it. So our guardians are the best citizens?"

"By far."

"And their wives will be the best women?"

"By far the best."

"And is there anything better for a State than having its men and women be as excellent as possible?"

"Nothing."

"And this is exactly what our program of music and gymnastics will achieve?"

"Certainly."

"Then our law is not only possible but supremely beneficial to the State."
elegant: Having established possibility, Socrates proves benefit: the same education produces excellent guardians regardless of sex. The best-educated guardians will be the best citizens, and having the best possible men and women serves the State's highest interest. The law is both possible and supremely beneficial.
summary: Socrates completes the argument for women's equality by establishing its benefit: equal education produces excellent guardians of both sexes, and the State's highest interest is served by having its men and women be as good as possible.
distilled: Equal education for women guardians is not only possible but maximally beneficial, producing the best possible citizens of both sexes.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The two-step method (possibility then benefit) structures Book V's argumentation -- The cobbler comparison recalls Book II's division of labor -- The conclusion that current practice violates nature while equal education fulfills it completes the first 'wave' of Book V -- This sets up the transition to the second wave: community of wives and children

narrative: "True. Then let the wives of our guardians strip, for their virtue will be their robe, and let them share in the toils of war and the defence of their country; only in the distribution of labours the lighter are to be assigned to the women, who are the weaker natures, but in other respects their duties are to be the same. And as for the man who laughs at naked women exercising their bodies from the best of motives, in his laughter he is plucking"
refashioned: "Then let the wives of our guardians train unclothed, for their virtue will be their garment. Let them share in the hardships of war and the defense of their country. In distributing tasks, the lighter duties should go to women, who are physically weaker, but in all other respects their responsibilities are to be the same. And the man who laughs at women exercising naked for the noblest reasons is, in his laughter, plucking --"
elegant: Women guardians should train and fight alongside men, clothed in virtue rather than fabric. Lighter physical tasks may be assigned to women given their lesser strength, but duties are otherwise identical. Anyone who mocks women exercising for noble purposes is a fool.
summary: Socrates declares that women guardians should exercise and serve in war alongside men, with virtue as their covering. Lighter tasks may accommodate physical differences, but duties are otherwise equal. Mockery of women training for noble ends is foolish.
distilled: Women guardians will share in warfare and training, with virtue as their robe and only lighter physical tasks as concession to lesser strength.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- 'Their virtue will be their robe' is one of the most famous lines from Book V -- The sentence is cut off mid-thought; it continues in 5.25 ('a fruit of unripe wisdom') -- The accommodation for physical strength differences shows Socrates' position is equal opportunity, not identical treatment -- This concludes the first wave with a practical policy statement

narrative:
"A fruit of unripe wisdom, and he himself is ignorant of what he is laughing at, or what he is about; -- for that is, and ever will be, the best of sayings, That the useful is the noble and the hurtful is the base."

"Very true."

"Here, then, is one difficulty in our law about women, which we may say that we have now escaped; the wave has not swallowed us up alive for enacting that the guardians of either sex should have all their pursuits in common; to the utility and also to the possibility of this arrangement the consistency of the argument with itself bears witness."

"Yes, that was a mighty wave which you have escaped."

"Yes," I said, "but a greater is coming; you will not think much of this when you see the next."

"Go on; let me see."

"The law," I said, "which is the sequel of this and of all that has preceded, is to the following effect, -- 'that the wives of our guardians are to be common, and their children are to be common, and no parent is to know his own child, nor any child his parent.'"

"Yes," he said, "that is a much greater wave than the other; and the possibility as well as the utility of such a law are far more questionable."

"I do not think," I said, "that there can be any dispute about the very great utility of having wives and children in common; the possibility is quite another matter, and will be very much disputed."

"I think that a good many doubts may be raised about both."

"You imply that the two questions must be combined," I replied. "Now I meant that you should admit the utility; and in this way, as I thought, I should escape from one of them, and then there would remain only the possibility."

"But that little attempt is detected, and therefore you will please to give a defence of both."
refashioned:
"-- a fruit of unripe wisdom. He doesn't even know what he's laughing at or why. For this is, and always will be, the best of maxims: the useful is the noble, and the harmful is the base."

"Very true."

"So we've survived the first difficulty in our law about women. The wave hasn't swallowed us for declaring that guardians of both sexes should share all pursuits in common. The argument's internal consistency confirms both the utility and possibility of the arrangement."

"That was indeed a mighty wave you escaped."

"Yes, but a greater one is coming. You won't think much of this first wave when you see the next."

"Go on -- let me see it."

"The next law, which follows from everything we've established, is this: the wives of our guardians are to be held in common, their children are to be held in common, and no parent is to know their own child, nor any child their parent."

"That is indeed a much greater wave! Both its possibility and utility are far more questionable."

"I don't think the utility of communal wives and children can seriously be disputed," I said. "The possibility is another matter entirely and will be hotly debated."

"I think serious doubts can be raised about both."

"You're insisting I address both questions together," I replied. "I was hoping you'd concede the utility so I'd only have to defend the possibility."

"Nice try -- but that little maneuver has been detected. You'll need to defend both."
elegant: The first wave -- equality of women -- is survived. Now comes the greater second wave: wives, children, and families held entirely in common, with no parent knowing their own child. Socrates claims the utility is obvious but the possibility debatable. Glaucon insists he must defend both.
summary: Having survived the first wave (women's equality), Socrates introduces the second and more controversial wave: complete community of wives and children, with no parent knowing their own child. He tries to separate utility from possibility, but Glaucon demands he defend both claims.
distilled: The second wave proposes abolishing the family: wives and children held in common, with no parent-child recognition.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'The useful is the noble and the hurtful is the base' connects the Greek concepts of to kalon (noble/beautiful) and to agathon (good) through utility -- The 'three waves' metaphor structures Book V: (1) women's equality, (2) community of families, (3) philosopher-kings -- The communal family proposal was influenced by Spartan practices but goes far beyond them -- This proposal was controversial even in antiquity; Aristotle criticizes it extensively in Politics II.1-5

narrative: "Well," I said, "I submit to my fate. Yet grant me a little favour: let me feast my mind with the dream as day dreamers are in the habit of feasting themselves when they are walking alone; for before they have discovered any means of effecting their wishes -- that is a matter which never troubles them -- they would rather not tire themselves by thinking about possibilities; but assuming that what they desire is already granted to them, they proceed with their plan, and delight in detailing what they mean to do when their wish has come true -- that is a way which they have of not doing much good to a capacity which was never good for much. Now I myself am beginning to lose heart, and I should like, with your permission, to pass over the question of possibility at present. Assuming therefore the possibility of the proposal, I shall now proceed to enquire how the rulers will carry out these arrangements, and I shall demonstrate that our plan, if executed, will be of the greatest benefit to the State and to the guardians. First of all, then, if you have no objection, I will endeavour with your help to consider the advantages of the measure; and hereafter the question of possibility."
refashioned:
"Very well, I submit. But grant me one favor: let me indulge in the dream the way daydreamers do when walking alone. They never bother figuring out how to achieve their wishes -- that's a concern that doesn't trouble them. They simply assume their desire is already fulfilled and proceed to happily detail what they'll do once it comes true. It's a habit that doesn't do much good for minds that weren't good for much to begin with.

I'm beginning to lose heart myself, so with your permission, I'd like to set aside the question of possibility for now. Assuming the proposal is possible, I'll examine how the rulers would carry out these arrangements and demonstrate that the plan, if executed, would bring the greatest benefit to the State and its guardians. Let me first address the advantages, and we'll tackle possibility afterward."
elegant: Socrates asks permission to assume possibility for now and focus on benefits -- like a daydreamer who skips over practical obstacles to envision ideal outcomes. He will show how rulers would implement the communal system and why it would benefit the State.
summary: Socrates asks to temporarily set aside the question of whether communal families are possible and instead demonstrate their benefits, comparing himself to a daydreamer who assumes his wish is granted and works out the details from there.
distilled: Socrates sets aside the question of possibility to first demonstrate the benefits of communal families, reasoning like a daydreamer.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The daydreamer metaphor is self-deprecating but also methodologically deliberate: Socrates separates the normative question (is this good?) from the practical question (is this possible?) -- This methodological move anticipates the later discussion of ideals versus reality (5.70-5.71) -- The question of possibility will eventually be addressed through the philosopher-king proposal (the third wave)

narrative:
"I have no objection; proceed."

"First, I think that if our rulers and their auxiliaries are to be worthy of the name which they bear, there must be willingness to obey in the one and the power of command in the other; the guardians must themselves obey the laws, and they must also imitate the spirit of them in any details which are entrusted to their care."
refashioned:
"No objection. Proceed."

"First, if our rulers and their auxiliaries are to deserve their titles, the auxiliaries must be willing to obey and the rulers must have the capacity to command. The guardians must obey the laws themselves, and in any matters entrusted to their discretion, they must act in the spirit of those laws."
elegant: Rulers must have the power to command and auxiliaries the willingness to obey. Guardians must both follow the laws and embody their spirit in discretionary matters.
summary: Socrates establishes the foundational principle for implementing communal families: rulers must command and auxiliaries must obey, with all guardians both following the laws and embodying their spirit.
distilled: The communal system requires rulers who command wisely and auxiliaries who obey willingly, all acting in the spirit of the laws.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between rulers (archontes) and auxiliaries (epikouroi) was established in Book III -- Rulers are the philosopher-guardians; auxiliaries are the warrior class -- 'Imitate the spirit' of the laws suggests principled discretion rather than rigid rule-following -- This sets up the need for the 'noble lie' about the marriage lottery

narrative:
"That is right," he said.

"You," I said, "who are their legislator, having selected the men, will now select the women and give them to them; -- they must be as far as possible of like natures with them; and they must live in common houses and meet at common meals. None of them will have anything specially his or her own; they will be together, and will be brought up together, and will associate at gymnastic exercises. And so they will be drawn by a necessity of their natures to have intercourse with each other -- necessity is not too strong a word, I think?"
refashioned:
"That's right," he said.

"You, as legislator, have already selected the men. Now you'll select women of similar natures and pair them together. They'll live in common houses and eat at common meals. No one will have private property. They'll live together, train together, and exercise together. And naturally, being together constantly, they'll be drawn by the necessity of their natures into sexual relationships -- and I don't think 'necessity' is too strong a word here."
elegant: The legislator selects men and women of like natures to live together in common houses with common meals, no private property, and shared training. Their constant proximity will naturally lead to sexual unions.
summary: Socrates outlines the communal living arrangements: men and women of similar natures selected to live in common houses, share meals, own nothing privately, and train together. Their close association will naturally lead to sexual relationships.
distilled: Guardians of both sexes will live communally with shared housing, meals, and training, naturally drawn into sexual unions by constant proximity.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Common meals (syssitia) were a real Spartan institution -- The abolition of private property for guardians was established in Book III (416d-417b) -- 'Necessity of their natures' (anagke) acknowledges eros as a natural force that must be channeled, not suppressed -- The legislator's role in pairing partners anticipates the regulated breeding program

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "-- necessity, not geometrical, but another sort of necessity which lovers know, and which is far more convincing and constraining to the mass of mankind."

"True," I said; "and this, Glaucon, like all the rest, must proceed after an orderly fashion; in a city of the blessed, licentiousness is an unholy thing which the rulers will forbid."

"Yes," he said, "and it ought not to be permitted."

"Then clearly the next thing will be to make matrimony sacred in the highest degree, and what is most beneficial will be deemed sacred?"

"Exactly."

"And how can marriages be made most beneficial? -- that is a question which I put to you, because I see in your house dogs for hunting, and of the nobler sort of birds not a few. Now, I beseech you, do tell me, have you ever attended to their pairing and breeding?"

"In what particulars?"

"Why, in the first place, although they are all of a good sort, are not some better than others?"

"True."

"And do you breed from them all indifferently, or do you take care to breed from the best only?"

"From the best."

"And do you take the oldest or the youngest, or only those of ripe age?"

"I choose only those of ripe age."

"And if care was not taken in the breeding, your dogs and birds would greatly deteriorate?"

"Certainly."

"And the same of horses and animals in general?"

"Undoubtedly."

"Good heavens! my dear friend," I said, "what consummate skill will our rulers need if the same principle holds of the human species!"

"Certainly, the same principle holds; but why does this involve any particular skill?"

"Because," I said, "our rulers will often have to practise upon the body corporate with medicines. Now you know that when patients do not require medicines, but have only to be put under a regimen, the inferior sort of practitioner is deemed to be good enough; but when medicine has to be given, then the doctor should be more of a man."

"That is quite true," he said; "but to what are you alluding?"
refashioned:
"Yes -- necessity," he said, "but not the geometric kind. This is the kind of necessity that lovers understand, and it's far more compelling to most people than any mathematical proof."

"True," I said. "And this natural attraction, Glaucon, must be regulated in an orderly fashion. In a city of the blessed, licentiousness is unholy and the rulers will forbid it."

"As they should."

"So the next step is to make marriage sacred to the highest degree. And what is most beneficial will be deemed most sacred?"

"Exactly."

"How can marriages be made most beneficial? I ask you this, Glaucon, because I know you keep hunting dogs and fine birds in your household. Tell me -- have you paid attention to their breeding?"

"In what way?"

"Though they're all good stock, aren't some better than others?"

"True."

"Do you breed from all of them indiscriminately, or only from the best?"

"Only from the best."

"And do you breed from the oldest or youngest, or only those in their prime?"

"Only those in their prime."

"And if you didn't breed carefully, your dogs and birds would deteriorate badly?"

"Certainly."

"The same goes for horses and all other animals?"

"Without question."

"Good heavens, my friend!" I said. "What extraordinary skill our rulers will need if the same principle applies to human beings!"

"The principle certainly applies. But why would this require special skill?"

"Because our rulers will frequently need to administer medicine to the body politic. When patients only need a healthy regimen, an ordinary practitioner will do. But when actual medicine must be administered, you need a far more capable physician."

"That's true. But what exactly are you getting at?"
elegant: Sexual attraction among communal guardians must be regulated, not left to license. Socrates draws an analogy to animal breeding: just as Glaucon breeds only from his best dogs and birds in their prime, the State must apply selective breeding to its guardians. This will require extraordinary skill from rulers -- the skill of a physician administering powerful medicine to the body politic.
summary: Socrates argues that the guardians' sexual unions must be carefully regulated, drawing an analogy to selective animal breeding. Just as a breeder mates only the best animals in their prime, the State must apply the same principle to human reproduction. This will require rulers of extraordinary skill, like physicians administering potent medicine.
distilled: Guardians' unions must be selectively regulated like animal breeding -- mating only the best in their prime -- requiring rulers of extraordinary skill.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The animal breeding analogy is central to Plato's eugenics proposal, which has been both influential and deeply controversial throughout history -- 'Necessity which lovers know' distinguishes erotic necessity (anagke erotike) from logical/mathematical necessity -- The 'medicine' metaphor foreshadows the 'noble lie' about the marriage lottery -- Glaucon's experience with hunting dogs and birds suggests his aristocratic background -- The breeding analogy was critiqued by Aristotle (Politics II.3-4) and has been controversial in modern reception

narrative:
"I mean," I replied, "that our rulers will find a considerable dose of falsehood and deceit necessary for the good of their subjects: we were saying that the use of all these things regarded as medicines might be of advantage."

"And we were very right."

"And this lawful use of them seems likely to be often needed in the regulations of marriages and births."
refashioned:
"I mean," I replied, "that our rulers will need a considerable amount of falsehood and deception for the good of their subjects. We established earlier that such things, used as medicines, can be beneficial."

"And we were right to say so."

"And this legitimate use of deception will likely be needed quite often in regulating marriages and births."
