elegant: Rulers will need falsehood and deception -- used as medicine -- to regulate marriages and births for the State's benefit. This 'noble lie' was previously justified as beneficial when administered by rulers.
summary: Socrates reveals that rulers will need to employ deception as a kind of medicine in regulating marriages and births, recalling the earlier justification for rulers' use of falsehood for the common good.
distilled: Rulers will use deception as beneficial medicine to regulate the guardians' marriages and breeding.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The justification of rulers' deception was established in Book III (389b-c) with the 'noble lie' (gennaion pseudos) -- This is one of the most controversial aspects of Plato's political philosophy -- The medical metaphor justifies paternalistic deception: just as doctors may deceive patients for their health, rulers may deceive citizens for the State's health -- Karl Popper's 'The Open Society and Its Enemies' famously attacked this Platonic doctrine -- The deception specifically involves rigging the marriage lottery to appear random

narrative:
"How so?"

"Why," I said, "the principle has been already laid down that the best of either sex should be united with the best as often, and the inferior with the inferior, as seldom as possible; and that they should rear the offspring of the one sort of union, but not of the other, if the flock is to be maintained in first-rate condition. Now these goings on must be a secret which the rulers only know, or there will be a further danger of our herd, as the guardians may be termed, breaking out into rebellion."
refashioned:
"How so?"

"The principle has already been established: the best men and women should be paired together as often as possible, and the inferior paired as seldom as possible. The offspring of the superior unions should be raised, but not those of the inferior -- if we want to maintain the guardian class in peak condition. These arrangements must remain secret, known only to the rulers; otherwise, the guardians -- our 'herd,' so to speak -- might rebel."
elegant: The best should breed with the best frequently, the inferior with the inferior rarely. Only superior offspring should be raised. These eugenic arrangements must remain secret to prevent rebellion among the guardians.
summary: Socrates states the eugenic principle plainly: the best guardians should be paired most frequently, the inferior least, with only superior offspring raised. This selective breeding must be kept secret from the guardians themselves to prevent unrest.
distilled: The best must breed with the best, inferior offspring must be removed, and the entire system must be kept secret to prevent rebellion.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage contains Plato's most explicit eugenics proposal -- 'Not rear the offspring of the other' is deliberately vague; it likely implies exposure (abandonment) of infants, a practice that existed in ancient Greece -- The secrecy requirement is essential: the system works only if guardians believe pairings are random -- The 'herd' (poimnion) and 'flock' metaphors reinforce the rulers-as-shepherds imagery -- This passage has been used both to celebrate and condemn Plato's political philosophy

narrative:
"Very true."

"Had we not better appoint certain festivals at which we will bring together the brides and bridegrooms, and sacrifices will be offered and suitable hymeneal songs composed by our poets: the number of weddings is a matter which must be left to the discretion of the rulers, whose aim will be to preserve the average of population? There are many other things which they will have to consider, such as the effects of wars and diseases and any similar agencies, in order as far as this is possible to prevent the State from becoming either too large or too small."
refashioned: "Shouldn't we establish regular festivals to bring together brides and bridegrooms, with sacrifices and wedding hymns composed by our poets? The number of marriages should be left to the rulers' discretion, since their goal will be to maintain a stable population. They'll need to account for many factors -- war casualties, disease, and similar forces -- to keep the State from growing too large or too small."
elegant: Regular marriage festivals with religious ceremony will bring couples together, with rulers controlling the number of marriages to maintain optimal population, accounting for losses from war and disease.
summary: Socrates proposes regular marriage festivals with sacrifices and hymns, where rulers control the number of unions to maintain stable population levels, accounting for losses from war and disease.
distilled: Marriage festivals regulated by rulers will maintain optimal population through controlled pairings, compensating for war and disease.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Hymeneal songs are wedding hymns, named after Hymen, the god of marriage -- Population management (maintaining neither too large nor too small a state) was a practical concern in Greek city-states -- This anticipates Aristotle's similar concerns about optimal population in Politics VII.4 -- The religious trappings (sacrifices, hymns) give legitimacy to what is essentially a state-managed breeding program

narrative:
"Certainly," he replied.

"We shall have to invent some ingenious kind of lots which the less worthy may draw on each occasion of our bringing them together, and then they will accuse their own ill-luck and not the rulers."

"To be sure," he said.

"And I think that our braver and better youth, besides their other honours and rewards, might have greater facilities of intercourse with women given them; their bravery will be a reason, and such fathers ought to have as many sons as possible."

"True."

"And the proper officers, whether male or female or both, for offices are to be held by women as well as by men --"

"Yes --"

"The proper officers will take the offspring of the good parents to the pen or fold, and there they will deposit them with certain nurses who dwell in a separate quarter; but the offspring of the inferior, or of the better when they chance to be deformed, will be put away in some mysterious, unknown place, as they should be."

"Yes," he said, "that must be done if the breed of the guardians is to be kept pure."
refashioned:
"We'll need to devise an ingenious lottery system so that the less worthy draw unfavorable lots at each mating festival. That way, they'll blame their own bad luck rather than the rulers."

"Of course," he said.

"And the braver and more excellent young guardians should receive, among their other honors and rewards, more frequent opportunities to mate. Their valor deserves it, and such fathers should have as many children as possible."

"True."

"The appropriate officials -- male, female, or both, since women hold office alongside men --"

"Yes --"

"These officials will take the children of the best parents to a communal nursery in a separate quarter, where designated nurses will care for them. But the offspring of inferior parents, or those of superior parents who are born deformed, will be quietly removed to some undisclosed place."

"Yes," he said, "that must be done to keep the guardian class pure."
elegant: A rigged lottery conceals the eugenic selection, making inferior guardians blame luck rather than rulers. The best warriors get more mating opportunities. Superior offspring go to communal nurseries; inferior or deformed children are removed to an unknown place.
summary: The rulers will rig a lottery so that inferior guardians blame bad luck for fewer mating opportunities. Brave warriors receive more chances to reproduce. Superior children are raised in communal nurseries, while inferior or deformed offspring are secretly removed.
distilled: A rigged lottery conceals eugenic selection; the bravest breed most, superior children are communally raised, and inferior offspring are secretly removed.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Put away in some mysterious, unknown place' almost certainly refers to infant exposure, a practiced (if controversial) reality in ancient Greece -- The rigged lottery is the specific mechanism of the 'noble lie' applied to breeding -- Officers 'male or female' maintains the principle of women's equality established in wave one -- 'Keeping the breed pure' uses animal husbandry language deliberately -- This is perhaps the most morally troubling passage in the Republic for modern readers

narrative: "They will provide for their nurture, and will bring the mothers to the fold when they are full of milk, taking the greatest possible care that no mother recognizes her own child; and other wet-nurses may be engaged if more are required. Care will also be taken that the process of suckling shall not be protracted too long; and the mothers will have no getting up at night or other trouble, but will hand over all this sort of thing to the nurses and attendants."
refashioned: "The officials will manage the children's care, bringing mothers to the nursery when their milk comes in, but taking the greatest care to ensure no mother recognizes her own child. Additional wet-nurses can be hired if needed. Nursing won't be prolonged unnecessarily, and the mothers will be spared night feedings and other burdens -- all of that will be handed over to the nurses and attendants."
elegant: Mothers will nurse infants at the communal nursery but must not recognize their own children. Wet-nurses supplement as needed. Mothers are freed from night feedings and childcare burdens, which fall to professional attendants.
summary: Mothers will be brought to the nursery to breastfeed but will be prevented from identifying their own children. Wet-nurses will supplement as needed, and mothers will be relieved of nighttime care and other burdens by professional attendants.
distilled: Mothers nurse at the communal nursery without knowing which child is theirs, freed from childcare burdens by professional nurses.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- The deliberate prevention of maternal recognition is essential to the communal family system: if mothers don't know their children, they'll treat all children as their own -- The relief of mothers from nighttime care could be read as either progressive (freeing women for guardian duties) or disturbing (severing the maternal bond) -- Aristotle criticizes this proposal in Politics II.3, arguing that 'watered-down' affection spread across all children is worse than concentrated natural affection

narrative:
"You suppose the wives of our guardians to have a fine easy time of it when they are having children."

"Why," said I, "and so they ought. Let us, however, proceed with our scheme. We were saying that the parents should be in the prime of life?"

"Very true."

"And what is the prime of life? May it not be defined as a period of about twenty years in a woman's life, and thirty in a man's?"

"Which years do you mean to include?"

"A woman," I said, "at twenty years of age may begin to bear children to the State, and continue to bear them until forty; a man may begin at five-and-twenty, when he has passed the point at which the pulse of life beats quickest, and continue to beget children until he be fifty-five."

"Certainly," he said, "both in men and women those years are the prime of physical as well as of intellectual vigour."
refashioned:
"It sounds like the guardian wives will have a pretty easy time of motherhood."

"And so they should," I said. "But let's continue. We were saying that parents should be in their prime?"

"Yes."

"And what is the prime of life? Roughly a twenty-year window for women and thirty years for men?"

"Which years exactly?"

"A woman may begin bearing children for the State at twenty and continue until forty. A man may begin at twenty-five -- once past the peak of youthful energy -- and continue until fifty-five."

"Certainly," he said. "For both men and women, those are the years of peak physical and intellectual vigor."
elegant: Women should bear children between ages twenty and forty, men between twenty-five and fifty-five -- the prime years of physical and intellectual vigor.
summary: Socrates defines the optimal childbearing period: women from age twenty to forty, men from twenty-five to fifty-five, corresponding to the prime of both physical and intellectual vigor.
distilled: The State prescribes childbearing ages: women from 20-40, men from 25-55, the years of peak physical and mental vigor.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- These age ranges roughly correspond to ancient Greek medical understanding of peak fertility -- 'The pulse of life beats quickest' (akme) refers to the Greek concept of physical/intellectual peak -- The different age ranges for men and women reflect ancient Greek assumptions about maturation -- 'Bear children to the State' emphasizes that reproduction is a civic duty, not a private matter -- Compare Aristotle's similar but different age recommendations in Politics VII.16

narrative:
"Any one above or below the prescribed ages who takes part in the public hymeneals shall be said to have done an unholy and unrighteous thing; the child of which he is the father, if it steals into life, will have been conceived under auspices very unlike the sacrifices and prayers, which at each hymeneal priestesses and priest and the whole city will offer, that the new generation may be better and more useful than their good and useful parents, whereas his child will be the offspring of darkness and strange lust."

"Very true," he replied.

"And the same law will apply to any one of those within the prescribed age who forms a connection with any woman in the prime of life without the sanction of the rulers; for we shall say that he is raising up a bastard to the State, uncertified and unconsecrated."
refashioned:
"Anyone outside the prescribed age range who participates in the public marriage rites will have committed an unholy and unrighteous act. Any child born from such a union -- if it manages to come into existence -- will have been conceived under omens entirely unlike the sacrifices and prayers that priestesses, priests, and the entire city offer at each marriage ceremony, praying that the next generation surpass their parents in goodness and usefulness. Such a child will be the offspring of darkness and illicit desire."

"Very true," he replied.

"And the same law applies to anyone of proper age who has relations with a woman in her prime without the rulers' approval. We'll declare that he is fathering an illegitimate child upon the State -- uncertified and unconsecrated."
elegant: Reproduction outside prescribed ages or without rulers' sanction is declared unholy. Authorized unions receive religious blessing -- prayers that children surpass their parents. Unauthorized children are deemed illegitimate offspring of 'darkness and strange lust,' uncertified by the State.
summary: Socrates declares that mating outside the prescribed ages or without rulers' approval is an unholy act. Sanctioned unions receive religious ceremony and prayers for superior offspring; unsanctioned children are considered illegitimate and unconsecrated.
distilled: Reproduction outside prescribed ages or without state sanction is declared unholy, producing illegitimate 'children of darkness.'
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The religious language (unholy, unconsecrated, sacrifices, prayers) gives eugenic regulation sacred authority -- 'Offspring of darkness and strange lust' contrasts with the blessed children of sanctioned unions -- The concept of state-certified reproduction centralizes reproductive control in the ruling class -- 'Uncertified and unconsecrated' (anenguon kai anieron) uses legal and religious terminology to stigmatize unauthorized reproduction

narrative:
"Very true," he replied.

"This applies, however, only to those who are within the specified age: after that we allow them to range at will, except that a man may not marry his daughter or his daughter's daughter, or his mother or his mother's mother; and women, on the other hand, are prohibited from marrying their sons or fathers, or son's son or father's father, and so on in either direction. And we grant all this, accompanying the permission with strict orders to prevent any embryo which may come into being from seeing the light; and if any force a way to the birth, the parents must understand that the offspring of such an union cannot be maintained, and arrange accordingly."

"That also," he said, "is a reasonable proposition. But how will they know who are fathers and daughters, and so on?"
refashioned:
"This restriction applies only during the prescribed childbearing years. After that, people may have relations freely -- except that a man may not be with his daughter, granddaughter, mother, or grandmother, and a woman may not be with her son, grandson, father, or grandfather. Even with this freedom, we'll give strict orders that no pregnancy resulting from these post-childbearing unions should come to term. And if a child is born despite these measures, the parents must understand that it cannot be raised."

"That seems reasonable," he said. "But how will they know who their fathers and daughters actually are?"
elegant: After the prescribed childbearing years, guardians may have free sexual relations with only incest prohibitions. Any resulting pregnancies must be terminated; any births must not be raised. Glaucon asks the obvious question: without family knowledge, how will they identify relatives?
summary: After childbearing age, guardians may have relations freely except with direct ancestors or descendants. Pregnancies from these unions must not come to term, and any resulting births must not be raised. Glaucon asks the critical question: how will anyone know who their relatives are?
distilled: Post-childbearing guardians may mate freely except for incest, but no resulting children may be raised.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Prevent any embryo from seeing the light' refers to abortion or infanticide -- The incest prohibition is complicated by the communal system where no one knows their actual parents -- Glaucon's question ('how will they know?') is astute and leads to the next passage's solution -- The tension between sexual freedom after childbearing age and strict control during it reveals the system's purely eugenic purpose -- Ancient Greek attitudes toward abortion and exposure differed significantly from modern ones

narrative: "They will never know. The way will be this: -- dating from the day of the hymeneal, the bridegroom who was then married will call all the male children who are born in the seventh and tenth month afterwards his sons, and the female children his daughters, and they will call him father, and he will call their children his grandchildren, and they will call the elder generation grandfathers and grandmothers. All who were begotten at the time when their fathers and mothers came together will be called their brothers and sisters, and these, as I was saying, will be forbidden to inter-marry. This, however, is not to be understood as an absolute prohibition of the marriage of brothers and sisters; if the lot favours them, and they receive the sanction of the Pythian oracle, the law will allow them."
refashioned: "They won't ever know for certain. Here's how it works: counting from the day of the marriage festival, the bridegroom will call all male children born in the seventh through tenth months his sons, and all female children his daughters. They will call him father, their children will be his grandchildren, and they will call the older generation grandfathers and grandmothers. Everyone conceived during the same mating period will be considered brothers and sisters, and will normally be forbidden to marry each other -- though this isn't an absolute prohibition. If the lottery favors them and the oracle at Delphi gives its sanction, the law will permit it."
elegant: Family identity is assigned by timing: all children born seven to ten months after a marriage festival are considered children of that cohort's parents. The resulting 'siblings' are generally forbidden to marry, unless approved by lot and the Delphic oracle.
summary: Since no one can know their actual parents, family relationships are assigned by timing: all children born within a certain window after a marriage festival are considered offspring of that cohort. These 'siblings' generally cannot intermarry, though exceptions can be made through lottery and oracle.
distilled: Family identity is assigned by birth timing relative to marriage festivals, creating cohort-based kinship without actual biological knowledge.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The seventh and tenth month window covers the range of normal gestation -- The Pythian oracle is the oracle at Delphi, dedicated to Apollo, the most authoritative religious institution in ancient Greece -- This system creates artificial but functionally real kinship bonds across the entire guardian class -- The exception for 'siblings' marrying with oracle approval provides flexibility -- Aristotle criticizes this as producing 'diluted' family bonds (Politics II.3-4)

narrative:
"Quite right," he replied.

"Such is the scheme, Glaucon, according to which the guardians of our State are to have their wives and families in common. And now you would have the argument show that this community is consistent with the rest of our polity, and also that nothing can be better -- would you not?"

"Yes, certainly."

"Shall we try to find a common basis by asking of ourselves what ought to be the chief aim of the legislator in making laws and in the organization of a State, -- what is the greatest good, and what is the greatest evil, and then consider whether our previous description has the stamp of the good or of the evil?"

"By all means."

"Can there be any greater evil than discord and distraction and plurality where unity ought to reign? or any greater good than the bond of unity?"

"There cannot."

"And there is unity where there is community of pleasures and pains -- where all the citizens are glad or grieved on the same occasions of joy and sorrow?"

"No doubt."

"Yes; and where there is no common but only private feeling a State is disorganized -- when you have one half of the world triumphing and the other plunged in grief at the same events happening to the city or the citizens?"

"Certainly."

"Such differences commonly originate in a disagreement about the use of the terms 'mine' and 'not mine,' 'his' and 'not his.'"

"Exactly so."

"And is not that the best-ordered State in which the greatest number of persons apply the terms 'mine' and 'not mine' in the same way to the same thing?"
refashioned:
"Quite right," he replied.

"That's the scheme, Glaucon: the guardians will share wives and families in common. Now you want to see that this arrangement is consistent with our overall political system and that nothing could be better?"

"Yes."

"Let's start by asking: what should be the legislator's chief aim? What is the greatest good for a State, and what is the greatest evil? Then we can judge whether our proposal bears the mark of good or evil."

"By all means."

"Is there any greater evil than discord, division, and fragmentation where unity should reign? Or any greater good than the bond of unity?"

"No."

"And unity exists where pleasures and pains are shared -- where all citizens rejoice and grieve together over the same events?"

"No doubt."

"Whereas a State is disorganized when feelings are private rather than shared -- when half the population celebrates while the other half mourns the very same events?"

"Certainly."

"Such divisions typically originate in disagreements about the words 'mine' and 'not mine,' 'his' and 'not his.'"

"Exactly."

"Then isn't the best-ordered State one in which the greatest number of people apply 'mine' and 'not mine' to the same things in the same way?"
elegant: The greatest evil in a State is discord where unity should reign; the greatest good is the bond of unity. Unity exists where pleasures and pains are communal. Division arises from private conceptions of 'mine' and 'not mine.' The best State is one where the most citizens share the same sense of 'mine.'
summary: Socrates argues that the State's greatest good is unity and greatest evil is discord. Unity means shared pleasures and pains; discord arises when citizens have competing private interests expressed through 'mine' and 'not mine.' The best-ordered State maximizes shared ownership and feeling.
distilled: The greatest good is civic unity through shared pleasures and pains; the greatest evil is discord from competing claims of 'mine' and 'not mine.'
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This argument for unity as the supreme political good is central to Plato's political philosophy -- The analysis of 'mine' and 'not mine' (to emon / to ouk emon) as the source of political division is remarkably modern -- Aristotle directly counters this in Politics II.2, arguing that some private ownership is necessary for generosity and that shared ownership leads to neglect -- The organic unity model (State as body) is developed in the next passage (5.40) -- This justification of communal property echoes the earlier abolition of private property for guardians (Book III)

narrative:
"Quite true."

"Or that again which most nearly approaches to the condition of the individual -- as in the body, when but a finger of one of us is hurt, the whole frame, drawn towards the soul as a center and forming one kingdom under the ruling power therein, feels the hurt and sympathizes all together with the part affected, and we say that the man has a pain in his finger; and the same expression is used about any other part of the body, which has a sensation of pain at suffering or of pleasure at the alleviation of suffering."
refashioned: "Or consider which State most resembles an individual person. When a finger is injured, the entire body -- organized as a single system under the soul's command -- feels the hurt and sympathizes with the affected part. We say 'I have a pain in my finger,' not 'my finger has a pain.' The whole person feels it. The same applies to any part of the body experiencing pain from injury or relief from healing."
elegant: The ideal State resembles an individual body: when a finger is hurt, the whole body feels it as one. We say 'I have a pain in my finger' -- the whole person sympathizes with the part. The best State achieves this same organic unity.
summary: Socrates compares the ideal State to an individual body: just as the whole body feels pain when a single finger is hurt, the best State should feel each citizen's joy or suffering as its own.
distilled: The ideal State is like a body that feels a finger's pain as its own -- each citizen's experience is the whole community's experience.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The body-politic analogy is one of the most influential political metaphors in Western thought -- 'The soul as a center' connects to the tripartite soul theory developed in Book IV -- This organic unity model contrasts with social contract theories that see the state as an aggregate of individuals -- The analogy influenced medieval political theology (the 'body of Christ' as church/state) and later thinkers like Hobbes (Leviathan as artificial person) -- Aristotle also uses organic analogies but draws different conclusions about the relationship between parts and whole

narrative:
"Very true," he replied; "and I agree with you that in the best-ordered State there is the nearest approach to this common feeling which you describe."

"Then when any one of the citizens experiences any good or evil, the whole State will make his case their own, and will either rejoice or sorrow with him?"

"Yes," he said, "that is what will happen in a well-ordered State."

"It will now be time," I said, "for us to return to our State and see whether this or some other form is most in accordance with these fundamental principles."

"Very good."

"Our State like every other has rulers and subjects?"

"True."

"All of whom will call one another citizens?"

"Of course."

"But is there not another name which people give to their rulers in other States?"

"Generally they call them masters, but in democratic States they simply call them rulers."

"And in our State what other name besides that of citizens do the people give the rulers?"

"They are called saviours and helpers," he replied.

"And what do the rulers call the people?"

"Their maintainers and foster-fathers."

"And what do they call them in other States?"

"Slaves."

"And what do the rulers call one another in other States?"

"Fellow-rulers."

"And what in ours?"

"Fellow-guardians."

"Did you ever know an example in any other State of a ruler who would speak of one of his colleagues as his friend and of another as not being his friend?"

"Yes, very often."

"And the friend he regards and describes as one in whom he has an interest, and the other as a stranger in whom he has no interest?"

"Exactly."

"But would any of your guardians think or speak of any other guardian as a stranger?"

"Certainly he would not; for every one whom they meet will be regarded by them either as a brother or sister, or father or mother, or son or daughter, or as the child or parent of those who are thus connected with him."
refashioned:
"I agree," he replied. "The best-ordered State achieves the closest approximation of this shared feeling."

"So when any citizen experiences good or ill fortune, the whole State will take it as their own, rejoicing or grieving together?"

"Yes, that's what happens in a well-ordered State."

"Then let's return to our State and see whether it matches these principles."

"Good."

"Our State, like every other, has rulers and subjects?"

"True."

"Who all call each other citizens?"

"Of course."

"But in other States, what do people call their rulers?"

"Usually 'masters.' In democracies, simply 'rulers.'"

"And in our State, what do the people call their rulers besides 'citizens'?"

"Saviors and helpers."

"And what do the rulers call the people?"

"Their providers and supporters."

"What do rulers call the people in other States?"

"Slaves."

"And what do rulers call each other in other States?"

"Fellow-rulers."

"And in ours?"

"Fellow-guardians."

"Have you ever seen a ruler in another State call one colleague a friend and another not a friend?"

"Frequently."

"Where the friend is someone he has a personal stake in, and the non-friend is a stranger he doesn't care about?"

"Exactly."

"But would any of our guardians treat another guardian as a stranger?"

"Never. Every guardian they encounter will be regarded as a brother, sister, father, mother, son, daughter, or the relative of someone who is."
elegant: In the ideal State, rulers are called 'saviors and helpers' and the people are called 'providers' -- not 'masters and slaves' as elsewhere. Rulers call each other 'fellow-guardians,' not 'fellow-rulers.' No guardian treats another as a stranger; all are family -- brothers, sisters, parents, or children.
summary: Socrates contrasts the ideal State's relationships with other governments. Where other states have masters and slaves, his State has saviors and providers. Where other rulers divide into friends and strangers, all guardians are family to one another -- no one is a stranger.
distilled: In the ideal State, rulers are saviors not masters, people are providers not slaves, and all guardians are family to one another.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The contrast between 'masters/slaves' and 'saviors/providers' reflects Plato's critique of tyranny and oligarchy -- 'Fellow-guardians' (symphylakes) emphasizes equality among the ruling class -- The universal family language (all are brothers, sisters, parents, children) is a direct consequence of the communal family system -- Democratic states calling rulers simply 'rulers' (archontes) may be an oblique reference to Athenian democracy -- The reciprocal terminology (saviors/providers) suggests a symbiotic rather than exploitative relationship between classes

narrative: "Capital," I said; "but let me ask you once more: Shall they be a family in name only; or shall they in all their actions be true to the name? For example, in the use of the word 'father,' would the care of a father be implied and the filial reverence and duty and obedience to him which the law commands; and is the violator of these duties to be regarded as an impious and unrighteous person who is not likely to receive much good either at the hands of God or of man? Are these to be or not to be the strains which the children will hear repeated in their ears by all the citizens about those who are intimated to them to be their parents and the rest of their kinsfolk?"
refashioned: "Excellent," I said. "But let me press the point: will they be a family in name only, or will their actions match the name? When someone is called 'father,' will that carry with it a father's care and a child's reverence, duty, and obedience -- as the law commands? Will anyone who violates these duties be considered impious and unrighteous, unlikely to receive good from gods or men? Will these be the constant messages that children hear from all citizens regarding those identified as their parents and relatives?"
elegant: The communal family must be real, not nominal: 'father' must carry actual paternal care, and 'child' must mean genuine filial duty and obedience. Violations must be treated as impiety. Children must constantly hear these family obligations reinforced by all citizens.
summary: Socrates insists the communal family must be substantive, not merely nominal. The titles of father, mother, brother, and sister must carry real obligations of care, reverence, and obedience, enforced by social pressure and religious sanction.
distilled: Communal family titles must carry real duties -- paternal care, filial obedience -- enforced by all citizens as sacred obligations.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The emphasis on substance over name connects to the broader Platonic concern with reality versus appearance -- 'Impious and unrighteous' (asebeia and adikia) are serious charges in Greek society; Socrates himself was convicted of impiety -- The constant repetition by 'all the citizens' creates a totalizing social reinforcement mechanism -- This passage addresses Aristotle's later objection that communal kinship would produce only diluted, nominal bonds

narrative:
"These," he said, "and none other; for what can be more ridiculous than for them to utter the names of family ties with the lips only and not to act in the spirit of them?"

"Then in our city the language of harmony and concord will be more often heard than in any other. As I was describing before, when any one is well or ill, the universal word will be 'with me it is well' or 'it is ill.'"

"Most true."

"And agreeably to this mode of thinking and speaking, were we not saying that they will have their pleasures and pains in common?"

"Yes, and so they will."

"And they will have a common interest in the same thing which they will alike call 'my own,' and having this common interest they will have a common feeling of pleasure and pain?"

"Yes, far more so than in other States."

"And the reason of this, over and above the general constitution of the State, will be that the guardians will have a community of women and children?"

"That will be the chief reason."

"And this unity of feeling we admitted to be the greatest good, as was implied in our own comparison of a well-ordered State to the relation of the body and the members, when affected by pleasure or pain?"

"That we acknowledged, and very rightly."

"Then the community of wives and children among our citizens is clearly the source of the greatest good to the State?"

"Certainly."

"And this agrees with the other principle which we were affirming, -- that the guardians were not to have houses or lands or any other property; their pay was to be their food, which they were to receive from the other citizens, and they were to have no private expenses; for we intended them to preserve their true character of guardians."
refashioned:
"Exactly," he said. "What could be more absurd than speaking the language of family without living by it?"

"Then in our city, the language of harmony and unity will be heard more than anywhere else. As I described before, when anyone prospers or suffers, the universal response will be 'things are going well for me' or 'things are going badly for me' -- everyone sharing the experience."

"Most true."

"And in keeping with this way of thinking, we said they'd share their pleasures and pains?"

"Yes, they will."

"And they'll have a common interest in the same things, which they'll all call 'my own.' Having this common interest, they'll share common feelings of pleasure and pain?"

"Far more so than in other States."

"And the chief reason for this, beyond the State's general constitution, is the community of women and children among the guardians?"

"That will be the main reason."

"And we agreed that this unity of feeling is the greatest good -- as shown by our comparison of a well-ordered State to a body that feels as one when any part is in pain or pleasure?"

"We did, and rightly so."

"Then the community of wives and children is clearly the source of the State's greatest good?"

"Certainly."

"And this is consistent with our earlier principle that guardians should have no houses, land, or private property -- that their compensation should be food provided by the other citizens, with no private expenses -- so they'd maintain their true character as guardians."
elegant: Family language without family practice would be absurd. The communal family system produces genuine shared feeling -- everyone's joy and suffering becomes everyone else's. This unity of feeling is the State's greatest good, consistent with the guardians' abolition of private property. Together, communal families and communal property preserve the guardians' true character.
summary: Socrates argues that the communal family system produces the State's greatest good: genuine unity of feeling where everyone shares in each person's joys and sorrows. This is consistent with the earlier abolition of private property for guardians, as both measures prevent the divisions caused by competing private interests.
distilled: Community of wives and children produces the State's greatest good -- unity of feeling -- complementing the abolition of private property.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The argument ties together two key reforms: communal families and communal property -- Both serve the same end: eliminating the 'mine/not mine' division that fractures states -- The guardians' compensation (food from other citizens) was established in Book III (416d-e) -- The body analogy from 5.40 is recalled and applied -- This represents the philosophical justification for the second wave's practical proposals

narrative:
"Right," he replied.

"Both the community of property and the community of families, as I am saying, tend to make them more truly guardians; they will not tear the city in pieces by differing about 'mine' and 'not mine;' each man dragging any acquisition which he has made into a separate house of his own, where he has a separate wife and children and private pleasures and pains; but all will be affected as far as may be by the same pleasures and pains because they are all of one opinion about what is near and dear to them, and therefore they all tend towards a common end."
refashioned:
"Right," he replied.

"Both communal property and communal families make them truer guardians. They won't tear the city apart by fighting over 'mine' and 'not mine' -- each person dragging their acquisitions into a separate household with a separate spouse and children, experiencing only private pleasures and pains. Instead, all guardians will be affected by the same joys and sorrows, because they share the same understanding of what matters to them, and so they all work toward a common end."
elegant: Communal property and communal families together make guardians truly worthy of the name. Without private households, spouses, and children to fight over, guardians share the same pleasures, pains, and purpose -- working as one toward a common end.
summary: Communal property and communal families together prevent guardians from tearing the city apart through competing private interests. Without separate households and families, they share the same experiences and work toward the same goals.
distilled: Communal property and communal families eliminate private interests, uniting guardians in shared purpose and feeling.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Dragging acquisitions into a separate house' vividly depicts the centrifugal force of private property -- This passage is the strongest statement of Plato's communism for the guardian class -- Note that communal property applies only to guardians, not to all citizens -- Aristotle's central criticism (Politics II.5) is that common ownership leads to neglect, not better care -- The phrase 'all tend towards a common end' (telos) connects political unity to the teleological framework of the Republic

narrative:
"Certainly," he replied.

"And as they have nothing but their persons which they can call their own, suits and complaints will have no existence among them; they will be delivered from all those quarrels of which money or children or relations are the occasion."

"Of course they will."

"Neither will trials for assault or insult ever be likely to occur among them. For that equals should defend themselves against equals we shall maintain to be honourable and right; we shall make the protection of the person a matter of necessity."

"That is good," he said.

"Yes; and there is a further good in the law; viz. that if a man has a quarrel with another he will satisfy his resentment then and there, and not proceed to more dangerous lengths."

"Certainly."

"To the elder shall be assigned the duty of ruling and chastising the younger."
refashioned:
"Since guardians own nothing but themselves, there'll be no lawsuits or complaints among them. They'll be free from all the quarrels that money, children, and family relationships cause."

"Of course."

"Trials for assault or insult will also be rare. We'll maintain that it's honorable and right for equals to defend themselves against equals, making self-defense a matter of duty."

"Good."

"And there's another benefit: if someone has a grievance with another, they'll settle it immediately rather than letting it escalate into something more dangerous."

"Certainly."

"And the elder will have the responsibility of supervising and disciplining the younger."
elegant: Without private property, guardians will have no lawsuits over money, children, or family disputes. Self-defense among equals is permitted and encouraged. Grievances are settled immediately to prevent escalation. Elders supervise and discipline the young.
summary: Since guardians own nothing privately, they'll be free from lawsuits and quarrels over property, money, and family. Self-defense among equals is encouraged, disputes are settled immediately, and elders have authority to discipline the young.
distilled: Without private property, guardians are freed from quarrels over money and family; disputes are settled immediately, with elders disciplining the young.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The elimination of lawsuits reflects Plato's critique of the litigious Athenian democracy -- Self-defense among equals (but not violence against elders) maintains honor while preventing abuse -- The immediate settlement of quarrels prevents the feuds that plagued Greek city-states -- The elder-younger authority structure reflects traditional Greek hierarchies preserved within the communal system -- This passage shows the practical benefits Socrates claims for communal living

narrative:
"Clearly. Nor can there be a doubt that the younger will not strike or do any other violence to an elder, unless the magistrates command him; nor will he slight him in any way. For there are two guardians, shame and fear, mighty to prevent him: shame, which makes men refrain from laying hands on those who are to them in the relation of parents; fear, that the injured one will be succoured by the others who are his brothers, sons, fathers."

"That is true," he replied.

"Then in every way the laws will help the citizens to keep the peace with one another?"

"Yes, there will be no want of peace."

"And as the guardians will never quarrel among themselves there will be no danger of the rest of the city being divided either against them or against one another."
refashioned:
"The young will never strike or disrespect an elder, unless commanded by a magistrate. Two powerful guardians prevent it: shame -- which keeps people from attacking those they regard as parents -- and fear -- that the injured party will be defended by others who consider themselves his brothers, sons, and fathers."

"That's true," he replied.

"So in every respect, these laws will help citizens maintain peace with one another?"

"There will be no shortage of peace."

"And since the guardians never quarrel among themselves, there'll be no risk of the rest of the city dividing against them or against each other."
elegant: Two forces prevent violence against elders: shame (they are regarded as parents) and fear (all others will defend them as family). Peace among guardians ensures peace throughout the city -- no internal division is possible.
summary: Shame and fear protect elders from youth violence: shame because all elders are treated as parents, and fear because all other citizens will rally to defend a family member. If guardians maintain peace among themselves, the entire city remains unified.
distilled: Shame and fear -- from universal family bonds -- prevent violence against elders and ensure peace throughout the city.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Shame and fear' (aidos and deos) are traditional Greek moral restraints, cited together in Homer and Hesiod -- The communal family system transforms private moral bonds into public ones: attacking any elder is attacking a 'parent' -- The political argument is that guardian unity guarantees social stability -- This recalls the principle from Book IV that justice in the city mirrors justice in the soul -- The claim that guardian harmony prevents all civic strife is arguably Plato's strongest practical argument for the communal system

narrative:
"None whatever. I hardly like even to mention the little meannesses of which they will be rid, for they are beneath notice: such, for example, as the flattery of the rich by the poor, and all the pains and pangs which men experience in bringing up a family, and in finding money to buy necessaries for their household, borrowing and then repudiating, getting how they can, and giving the money into the hands of women and slaves to keep -- the many evils of so many kinds which people suffer in this way are mean enough and obvious enough, and not worth speaking of."

"Yes, he said, a man has no need of eyes in order to perceive that."

"And from all these evils they will be delivered, and their life will be blessed as the life of Olympic victors and yet more blessed."
refashioned:
"I hardly want to mention the petty miseries they'll be freed from, since they're beneath serious discussion: the way the poor flatter the rich, all the anxieties of raising a family, scrambling to find money for household necessities, borrowing and defaulting, scraping together whatever they can, handing money over to wives and slaves to manage -- the countless mean little sufferings that people endure. These are too obvious and too trivial to dwell on."

"Yes," he said, "you don't need eyes to see that."

"Freed from all these miseries, the guardians will live a life as blessed as Olympic victors -- and even more so."
elegant: Guardians will also be freed from the petty miseries of ordinary life: flattering the wealthy, scrambling for money, family financial anxieties, borrowing and defaulting. Their blessed life will surpass even that of Olympic victors.
summary: Beyond the great political benefits, guardians will be freed from everyday miseries: financial anxiety, servile flattery, the scramble to support a household. Their life will be even more blessed than that of Olympic victors.
distilled: Guardians are freed from petty financial anxieties and domestic miseries, achieving a life more blessed than Olympic victors.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Olympic victors received free meals at public expense in many Greek cities -- The list of domestic miseries reflects actual conditions of Athenian household life -- 'Giving money into the hands of women and slaves' reveals the gendered household economy Socrates proposes to abolish -- The comparison to Olympic victors sets up the argument in 5.48 that guardians' rewards exceed athletic prizes -- Socrates' dismissal of these concerns as 'beneath notice' is somewhat ironic given their importance to ordinary citizens

narrative:
"How so?"

"The Olympic victor," I said, "is deemed happy in receiving a part only of the blessedness which is secured to our citizens, who have won a more glorious victory and have a more complete maintenance at the public cost. For the victory which they have won is the salvation of the whole State; and the crown with which they and their children are crowned is the fulness of all that life needs; they receive rewards from the hands of their country while living, and after death have an honourable burial."
refashioned:
"How so?"

"Olympic victors are considered blessed for receiving only a fraction of what our guardians enjoy. The guardians have won a far more glorious victory and receive far more complete public support. Their victory is nothing less than the salvation of the entire State. Their crown is the fullness of everything life requires. They receive their country's rewards while alive and an honorable burial after death."
elegant: Guardians surpass Olympic victors: their victory is the State's salvation, their crown is the fullness of life's needs, and they receive both living honors and honorable burial.
summary: Socrates argues that guardians are more blessed than Olympic victors because their victory is the salvation of the entire State, their reward is everything life requires, and they receive both civic honors in life and honorable burial in death.
distilled: Guardians win a greater victory than Olympic champions -- the salvation of the State -- and receive the fullness of life's needs as their crown.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Olympic victors received significant honors: free meals (sitesis), front-row seats at festivals, sometimes substantial prizes -- The comparison elevates political service above athletic achievement -- 'Salvation of the whole State' (soteria) uses religious/military language -- 'Honourable burial' connects to Greek heroic tradition where proper burial was essential -- This passage responds to Adeimantus's earlier objection (Book IV, 419a) that guardians seem to get a raw deal

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and glorious rewards they are."

"Do you remember," I said, "how in the course of the previous discussion some one who shall be nameless accused us of making our guardians unhappy -- they had nothing and might have possessed all things -- to whom we replied that, if an occasion offered, we might perhaps hereafter consider this question, but that, as at present advised, we would make our guardians truly guardians, and that we were fashioning the State with a view to the greatest happiness, not of any particular class, but of the whole?"

"Yes, I remember."

"And what do you say, now that the life of our protectors is made out to be far better and nobler than that of Olympic victors -- is the life of shoemakers, or any other artisans, or of husbandmen, to be compared with it?"
refashioned:
"Yes, glorious rewards indeed."

"Do you remember," I said, "when someone -- who shall remain nameless -- accused us of making the guardians unhappy because they had nothing when they could have had everything? We replied that we might revisit the question later, but for now, our goal was to make the guardians truly guardians, designing the State for the greatest happiness of the whole, not any particular class."

"Yes, I remember."

"So now that the guardians' life is shown to be far better and nobler than Olympic victors' -- can the life of shoemakers, artisans, or farmers really be compared to it?"
elegant: Socrates recalls the earlier objection that guardians are made unhappy by their poverty. Now that their life is shown to surpass even Olympic victors, the objection falls away. The State is designed for total happiness, not any single class's comfort.
summary: Socrates recalls the earlier accusation that guardians are unhappy because they own nothing. Now that their life has been shown to surpass Olympic victors, the objection dissolves -- the State is designed for the whole community's happiness, not any one class.
distilled: The earlier objection that guardians are unhappy is refuted: their life surpasses Olympic victors, and the State serves the whole, not one class.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The 'nameless' accuser is Adeimantus, who raised this objection in Book IV (419a-420a) -- The principle of designing for whole-state happiness rather than class happiness is central to the Republic's political theory -- The comparison with artisans and farmers reinforces the three-class structure (rulers, warriors, producers) -- This passage resolves a hanging question from Book IV and demonstrates the dialogue's careful architecture

narrative:
"Certainly not."

"At the same time I ought here to repeat what I have said elsewhere, that if any of our guardians shall try to be happy in such a manner that he will cease to be a guardian, and is not content with this safe and harmonious life, which, in our judgment, is of all lives the best, but infatuated by some youthful conceit of happiness which gets up into his head shall seek to appropriate the whole State to himself, then he will have to learn how wisely Hesiod spoke, when he said, 'half is more than the whole.'"

"If he were to consult me, I should say to him: Stay where you are, when you have the offer of such a life."
refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"But I should repeat what I've said before: if any guardian tries to pursue happiness in a way that makes him cease being a guardian -- if he's not content with this safe and harmonious life, which we consider the best of all lives, but becomes intoxicated by some youthful fantasy of happiness and tries to seize the entire State for himself -- then he'll discover the wisdom of Hesiod's saying: 'Half is more than the whole.'"

"If he asked my advice, I'd tell him: stay right where you are. You won't find a better life than the one being offered."
elegant: Any guardian who abandons his role to seize power for personal happiness will learn Hesiod's lesson: 'half is more than the whole.' The guardian life, though modest in possessions, is the best possible life.
summary: Socrates warns that any guardian who abandons his role to seize personal power will learn Hesiod's wisdom that 'half is more than the whole.' The guardian's modest but harmonious life is the best possible existence.
distilled: A guardian who abandons duty for personal power discovers that 'half is more than the whole' -- the modest guardian life is the best life.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Hesiod's 'half is more than the whole' comes from Works and Days (40) -- The warning against guardians seizing power for themselves anticipates the discussion of tyranny in Books VIII-IX -- 'Youthful conceit of happiness' suggests the appetitive soul overcoming reason -- This connects to the tyrant's psychology analyzed in Book IX, where the tyrant is shown to be the most miserable of men -- The guardian's 'safe and harmonious life' represents the philosophical life of reason governing appetite

narrative: "You agree then," I said, "that men and women are to have a common way of life such as we have described -- common education, common children; and they are to watch over the citizens in common whether abiding in the city or going out to war; they are to keep watch together, and to hunt together like dogs; and always and in all things, as far as they are able, women are to share with the men? And in so doing they will do what is best, and will not violate, but preserve the natural relation of the sexes."
refashioned: "Then you agree," I said, "that men and women will share a common way of life as we've described: common education, common children, common responsibility for watching over the citizens whether in peacetime or war. They'll stand guard together and hunt together like dogs. In all things, as far as they are able, women will share equally with men. In doing so, they'll be doing what is best, and they'll preserve rather than violate the natural relationship between the sexes."
elegant: Men and women will share everything: education, children, civic duty, military service. Women share with men in all things as far as they are able. This preserves, rather than violates, the natural relation between the sexes.
summary: Socrates summarizes the agreement: men and women share common education, children, civic duties, and military service. Women participate in everything as far as they are able. This arrangement preserves rather than violates the natural relationship between the sexes.
distilled: Complete sharing between male and female guardians -- in education, children, and duty -- preserves rather than violates nature.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage summarizes both waves one (women's equality) and two (communal families) -- The dog/hunting analogy returns from 5.9 -- 'As far as they are able' acknowledges the physical strength differential while maintaining equality of role -- The insistence that this 'preserves the natural relation' directly inverts the conventional Greek view that women's confinement to the household was natural -- This sets up the transition to the third and greatest wave: philosopher-kings

narrative:
"I agree with you," he replied.

"The enquiry," I said, "has yet to be made, whether such a community be found possible -- as among other animals, so also among men -- and if possible, in what way possible?"

"You have anticipated the question which I was about to suggest."

"There is no difficulty," I said, "in seeing how war will be carried on by them."
refashioned:
"I agree," he replied.

"We still need to determine whether such a community is actually possible -- among humans as it is among other animals -- and if so, in what way."

"You've anticipated my next question."

"There's no difficulty in seeing how they'll conduct warfare."
elegant: The question of possibility remains: can this communal system work among humans as it does among animals? Socrates transitions to the practical question of how guardians will wage war.
summary: Glaucon agrees with the communal system but notes the question of possibility remains unanswered. Socrates acknowledges this and transitions to discussing how the guardians will conduct warfare.
distilled: The possibility question remains open as Socrates turns to the practical matter of how communal guardians will wage war.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'As among other animals, so also among men' recalls the animal breeding analogy from 5.29 -- The question of possibility will ultimately be addressed by the philosopher-king proposal (third wave) -- The transition to warfare serves as a practical digression before the main philosophical climax -- Glaucon's 'anticipated the question' shows the natural flow of dialectical inquiry

narrative:
"How?"

"Why, of course they will go on expeditions together; and will take with them any of their children who are strong enough, that, after the manner of the artisan's child, they may look on at the work which they will have to do when they are grown up; and besides looking on they will have to help and be of use in war, and to wait upon their fathers and mothers. Did you never observe in the arts how the potters' boys look on and help, long before they touch the wheel?"
refashioned:
"How will they fight?"

"They'll go on military campaigns together, of course, and bring along any children strong enough to observe. Just as artisans' children learn by watching their parents work, these children will observe the warfare they'll eventually practice. Beyond just watching, they'll assist in the war effort and serve their fathers and mothers. Haven't you noticed how potters' children watch and help long before they ever touch the wheel themselves?"
elegant: Guardian children will accompany military expeditions to observe and assist, learning warfare through apprenticeship -- just as potters' children watch and help long before touching the wheel.
summary: Guardian children strong enough to observe will accompany military expeditions, learning warfare through apprenticeship as artisans' children learn their craft -- watching and helping before practicing independently.
distilled: Guardian children will learn warfare through direct observation on campaigns, just as potters' children learn by watching before practicing.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The apprenticeship model for military training through observation is practical and reflects actual Greek craft education -- The potter's wheel analogy is concrete and relatable -- 'Wait upon their fathers and mothers' -- note both parents mentioned, consistent with women's military role -- This practical detail about education through observation connects to the broader theme of experiential learning -- Spartan education (agoge) included similar elements of youth military exposure

narrative:
"Yes, I have."

"And shall potters be more careful in educating their children and in giving them the opportunity of seeing and practising their duties than our guardians will be?"

"The idea is ridiculous," he said.

"There is also the effect on the parents, with whom, as with other animals, the presence of their young ones will be the greatest incentive to valour."

"That is quite true, Socrates; and yet if they are defeated, which may often happen in war, how great the danger is! the children will be lost as well as their parents, and the State will never recover."

"True," I said; "but would you never allow them to run any risk?"

"I am far from saying that."

"Well, but if they are ever to run a risk should they not do so on some occasion when, if they escape disaster, they will be the better for it?"

"Clearly."

"Whether the future soldiers do or do not see war in the days of their youth is a very important matter, for the sake of which some risk may fairly be incurred."

"Yes, very important."

"This then must be our first step, -- to make our children spectators of war; but we must also contrive that they shall be secured against danger; then all will be well."

"True."

"Their parents may be supposed not to be blind to the risks of war, but to know, as far as human foresight can, what expeditions are safe and what dangerous?"

"That may be assumed."

"And they will take them on the safe expeditions and be cautious about the dangerous ones?"

"True."

"And they will place them under the command of experienced veterans who will be their leaders and teachers?"

"Very properly."

"Still, the dangers of war cannot be always foreseen; there is a good deal of chance about them?"

"True."

"Then against such chances the children must be at once furnished with wings, in order that in the hour of need they may fly away and escape."
refashioned:
"Surely our guardians won't be less diligent about their children's education than potters are about theirs?"

"That would be absurd."

"And having children present on campaign will inspire parents to greater courage, just as it does with other animals."

"True, Socrates. But if they're defeated -- which happens often in war -- the danger is enormous. The children could be lost along with their parents, and the State might never recover."

"True. But would you never allow them any risk at all?"

"I'm not saying that."

"If they're going to face risk, shouldn't it be in situations where surviving the danger makes them better for it?"

"Of course."

"Whether future soldiers witness war in their youth is extremely important -- important enough to justify some risk."

"Agreed."

"So our first step is to make children spectators of war, while also ensuring they're protected from danger."

"True."

"Their parents won't be blind to the risks. They'll know, as far as human foresight allows, which campaigns are safe and which are dangerous."

"We can assume that."

"They'll bring children only on safe expeditions and keep them away from dangerous ones?"

"True."

"And they'll place children under the command of experienced veterans who serve as their leaders and teachers?"

"Quite right."

"But the fortunes of war can't always be predicted -- there's always an element of chance."

"True."

"So we must give the children 'wings' -- the means to flee and escape if things go wrong."
elegant: Children should observe war to learn courage, with parents' presence inspiring valor. The risks are managed through careful selection of safe expeditions, experienced veteran supervision, and escape plans ('wings') for emergencies. Some risk is justified by the educational value.
summary: Guardian children should observe warfare, with parental presence inspiring courage. Risks are managed through careful expedition selection, veteran supervision, and emergency escape provisions. Though the danger of losing children is real, the educational benefit justifies some carefully managed risk.
distilled: Children should observe war under careful protection -- parental presence inspires courage, and managed risk is justified by educational value.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's practical objection (what if they lose?) shows his military awareness -- The 'wings' metaphor is explained in the next passage as fast horses -- The risk-benefit calculation is pragmatic and military in character -- Spartan youth education included battlefield observation -- The argument that managed risk-taking is essential to education connects to broader Platonic themes about courage (andreia) as discussed in Book IV -- The veteran-mentor system resembles the older erastes-eromenos educational relationship in Greek culture

narrative:
"What do you mean?" he said.

"I mean that we must mount them on horses in their earliest youth, and when they have learnt to ride, take them on horseback to see war: the horses must not be spirited and warlike, but the most tractable and yet the swiftest that can be had. In this way they will get an excellent view of what is hereafter to be their own business; and if there is danger they have only to follow their elder leaders and escape."
refashioned:
"What do you mean by 'wings'?"

"I mean we must teach them to ride horses from an early age. Once they can ride, we'll take them on horseback to observe warfare. The horses should be gentle and obedient, yet the fastest available -- not spirited warhorses. This way, the children get an excellent view of the work they'll eventually do themselves. And if danger arises, they simply follow their veteran leaders and ride away to safety."
elegant: Children's 'wings' are fast, gentle horses. Trained to ride early, children observe battles from horseback on tractable but swift mounts, able to flee danger by following their veteran leaders.
summary: Socrates explains that children will be given fast but gentle horses as their 'wings' for observing warfare. Taught to ride from an early age, they can watch battles safely and escape quickly by following veteran leaders if danger arises.
distilled: Children observe war from fast, gentle horses -- able to flee if danger arises -- learning their future profession through safe observation.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The emphasis on tractable rather than warlike horses shows practical concern for children's safety -- Horsemanship (hippike) was an aristocratic skill in Athens; the cavalry class (hippeis) was the second-highest census class -- 'What is hereafter to be their own business' reinforces the apprenticeship model -- The practical detail of horse selection shows Socrates engaging seriously with military logistics -- This connects to the broader theme that education must involve direct experience, not just abstract learning

narrative:
"I believe that you are right," he said.

"Next, as to war; what are to be the relations of your soldiers to one another and to their enemies? I should be inclined to propose that the soldier who leaves his rank or throws away his arms, or is guilty of any other act of cowardice, should be degraded into the rank of a husbandman or artisan. What do you think?"
refashioned:
"I think you're right," he said.

"Now, regarding warfare: what should be the relations among soldiers, and between soldiers and their enemies? I'd propose that any soldier who abandons his position, throws down his weapons, or commits any other act of cowardice should be demoted to the rank of farmer or artisan. What do you think?"
elegant: Cowardice in battle -- abandoning position, dropping weapons -- should be punished by demotion from guardian to the producer class (farmer or artisan).
summary: Socrates proposes that any soldier guilty of cowardice -- leaving their post, abandoning weapons -- should be demoted from the guardian class to the rank of farmer or artisan.
distilled: Cowardly soldiers should be demoted from guardian to producer class as punishment.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- Demotion between classes shows the class system is somewhat permeable, not entirely hereditary -- In Athenian law, throwing away one's shield (rhipsaspia) was a serious offense that could result in loss of citizenship -- The three-class system (guardians, auxiliaries, producers) was established in Books II-III -- This punishment converts a guardian into a member of the lowest class, a severe social degradation -- Compare Spartan customs where returning without one's shield was deeply shameful ('with your shield or on it')

narrative:
"By all means, I should say."

"And he who allows himself to be taken prisoner may as well be made a present of to his enemies; he is their lawful prey, and let them do what they like with him."

"Certainly."

"But the hero who has distinguished himself, what shall be done to him? In the first place, he shall receive honour in the army from his youthful comrades; every one of them in succession shall crown him. What do you say?"

"I approve."

"And what do you say to his receiving the right hand of fellowship?"
refashioned:
"Absolutely."

"And whoever allows himself to be captured may as well be handed over to his enemies as a gift. He's their lawful prize -- let them do what they will with him."

"Certainly."

"But what about the hero who distinguishes himself? First, he should receive honors from his younger comrades in the army -- each one crowning him in turn. Agreed?"

"I approve."

"And should he also receive handshakes of fellowship from all?"
elegant: Captured soldiers are abandoned to their enemies. Heroes, by contrast, are crowned by comrades and receive the fellowship of the army.
summary: Soldiers who allow themselves to be captured are abandoned to the enemy. Those who distinguish themselves in battle are honored by their comrades, crowned in succession, and offered the hand of fellowship.
distilled: Captured soldiers are surrendered to the enemy; heroes are crowned and honored by their comrades.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The harsh treatment of prisoners reflects the Greek warrior ethos where capture was considered deeply shameful -- Compare the Spartan ideal that warriors should fight to the death rather than surrender -- Crowning (stephanos) was a standard Greek honor, used in both athletic and military contexts -- The 'right hand of fellowship' (dexiosis) was a formal gesture of solidarity and acceptance -- These honors serve both to reward courage and to incentivize bravery through social recognition

narrative:
"To that too, I agree."

"But you will hardly agree to my next proposal."

"What is your proposal?"

"That he should kiss and be kissed by them."

"Most certainly, and I should be disposed to go further, and say: Let no one whom he has a mind to kiss refuse to be kissed by him while the expedition lasts. So that if there be a lover in the army, whether his love be youth or maiden, he may be more eager to win the prize of valour."
refashioned:
"I agree to that as well."

"But you'll probably resist my next proposal."

"What is it?"

"That the hero should kiss and be kissed by his comrades."

"I not only agree -- I'd go further. While the campaign lasts, no one the hero wishes to kiss may refuse him. This way, if there's a lover in the army -- whether their beloved is a young man or a young woman -- they'll have even greater motivation to win the prize of valor."
elegant: Heroes earn the right to kiss their comrades, and Glaucon goes further: during campaigns, no one may refuse a hero's kiss. This uses erotic motivation to incentivize military valor.
summary: Socrates proposes that heroes should earn kisses from comrades as honors. Glaucon enthusiastically agrees and adds that no one should refuse a hero's kiss during a campaign, using romantic motivation to incentivize bravery.
distilled: Heroes earn the right to kiss any comrade during campaigns, using erotic incentive to drive military valor.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Whether his love be youth or maiden' acknowledges both heterosexual and homosexual eros as military motivators -- The Sacred Band of Thebes (formed c. 378 BCE, around the time of the Republic) was an elite unit of male lovers whose devotion to each other made them formidable warriors -- Glaucon's enthusiasm here plays on his characterization as a lover of beauty (established in 5.77) -- The connection between eros and martial courage (andreia) was common in Greek thought -- Plato's Symposium (178d-179a) discusses how love inspires battlefield heroism

narrative:
"Capital," I said.

"That the brave man is to have more wives than others has been already determined: and he is to have first choices in such matters more than others, in order that he may have as many children as possible?"

"Agreed."

"Again, there is another manner in which, according to Homer, brave youths should be honoured; for he tells how Ajax, after he had distinguished himself in battle, was rewarded with long chines, which seems to be a compliment appropriate to a hero in the flower of his age, being not only a tribute of honour but also a very strengthening thing."

"Most true," he said.

"Then in this," I said, "Homer shall be our teacher; and we too, at sacrifices and on the like occasions, will honour the brave according to the measure of their valour, whether men or women, with hymns and those other distinctions which we were mentioning; also with seats of precedence, and meats and full cups; and in honouring them, we shall be at the same time training them."

"That," he replied, "is excellent."

"Yes," I said; "and when a man dies gloriously in war shall we not say, in the first place, that he is of the golden race?"

"To be sure."

"Nay, have we not the authority of Hesiod for affirming that when they are dead 'They are holy angels upon the earth, authors of good, averters of evil, the guardians of speech-gifted men?'"

"Yes; and we accept his authority."

"We must learn of the god how we are to order the sepulture of divine and heroic personages, and what is to be their special distinction; and we must do as he bids?"

"By all means."

"And in ages to come we will reverence them and kneel before their sepulchres as at the graves of heroes. And not only they but any who are deemed pre-eminently good, whether they die from age, or in any other way, shall be admitted to the same honours."

"That is very right," he said.

"Next, how shall our soldiers treat their enemies? What about this?"
refashioned:
"Excellent," I said.

"We've already established that brave men should have more mating opportunities than others and first choice in these matters, so they produce as many children as possible."

"Agreed."

"Homer offers another model for honoring brave youth. He tells how Ajax, after distinguishing himself in battle, was rewarded with prime cuts of meat -- an honor fitting for a hero in his prime, being both a tribute and a source of strength."

"Very true."

"So Homer will be our guide. At sacrifices and festivals, we'll honor the brave according to their valor -- men and women alike -- with hymns, distinctions, seats of honor, choice meats, and full cups. In honoring them, we'll simultaneously be training them."

"Excellent."

"And when someone dies gloriously in war, shall we not declare them to be of the golden race?"

"Certainly."

"Don't we have Hesiod's authority for saying that when they die, 'They become holy spirits upon the earth, authors of good, averters of evil, guardians of mortal men'?"

"Yes, and we accept his authority."

"We must consult the god about how to conduct funerals for these divine and heroic persons, and what special honors they should receive."

"By all means."

"In ages to come, we'll reverence them and honor their tombs as heroes' graves. And not just the war dead -- anyone deemed exceptionally good, whether they die of old age or any other cause, should receive the same honors."

"That's absolutely right."

"Next, how should our soldiers treat their enemies?"
elegant: Heroes receive comprehensive rewards: more mating opportunities, choice food (following Homer's precedent with Ajax), hymns, seats of honor, and full cups -- for men and women alike. Those who die gloriously join Hesiod's 'golden race' as guardian spirits, receiving heroic funerals and eternal veneration. Even the peacefully deceased, if exceptionally virtuous, earn the same honors.
summary: Socrates outlines a comprehensive honor system for heroes: more mating rights, Homeric feasts, hymns, and precedence -- for both men and women. The gloriously fallen are elevated to Hesiod's golden race as guardian spirits with heroic burials, and all exceptionally good citizens receive the same honors regardless of how they die.
distilled: Heroes receive more mates, feasts, hymns, and honors in life; in death they become guardian spirits, venerated as Hesiod's golden race.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Ajax's reward of 'long chines' (prime back-cuts of meat) comes from Iliad 7.321 -- Hesiod's 'golden race' from Works and Days (121-126) describes the first generation of mortals who became benevolent daimones after death -- 'Whether men or women' again reinforces gender equality in the guardian class -- 'Learn of the god' refers to consulting the Delphic oracle about funeral rites -- The honor system serves dual purposes: rewarding excellence and incentivizing virtue -- The transition to treating enemies shifts from internal to external military ethics

narrative:
