"In what respect do you mean?"

"First of all, in regard to slavery? Do you think it right that Hellenes should enslave Hellenic States, or allow others to enslave them, if they can help? Should not their custom be to spare them, considering the danger which there is that the whole race may one day fall under the yoke of the barbarians?"
refashioned:
"In what respect?"

"First, regarding slavery. Is it right for Greeks to enslave other Greek states, or to allow others to enslave them? Shouldn't the custom be to spare fellow Greeks, considering the danger that the entire Greek race might someday fall under barbarian domination?"
elegant: Greeks should not enslave fellow Greeks, given the constant threat that the entire Greek race could fall under barbarian rule. Pan-Hellenic solidarity demands mutual protection.
summary: Socrates raises the question of whether Greeks should enslave other Greeks, arguing they should spare fellow Hellenes given the ever-present threat of barbarian domination over the whole race.
distilled: Greeks should not enslave fellow Greeks, since barbarian domination threatens the entire race.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The pan-Hellenic sentiment reflects growing Greek consciousness of shared identity, especially after the Persian Wars (490-479 BCE) -- 'Barbarians' primarily refers to Persians but includes all non-Greek peoples -- This prohibition of enslaving Greeks while implicitly accepting the enslavement of non-Greeks reveals the limits of Plato's universalism -- The concern about barbarian domination was prescient: Macedonian hegemony would come within decades of the Republic's composition -- Compare Isocrates' pan-Hellenic advocacy in his speeches

narrative:
"To spare them is infinitely better."

"Then no Hellene should be owned by them as a slave; that is a rule which they will observe and advise the other Hellenes to observe."

"Certainly," he said; "they will in this way be united against the barbarians and will keep their hands off one another."

"Next as to the slain; ought the conquerors, I said, to take anything but their armour? Does not the practice of despoiling an enemy afford an excuse for not facing the battle? Cowards skulk about the dead, pretending that they are fulfilling a duty, and many an army before now has been lost from this love of plunder."

"Very true."

"And is there not illiberality and avarice in robbing a corpse, and also a degree of meanness and womanishness in making an enemy of the dead body when the real enemy has flown away and left only his fighting gear behind him, -- is not this rather like a dog who cannot get at his assailant, quarrelling with the stones which strike him instead?"

"Very like a dog," he said.

"Then we must abstain from spoiling the dead or hindering their burial?"

"Yes," he replied, "we most certainly must."

"Neither shall we offer up arms at the temples of the gods, least of all the arms of Hellenes, if we care to maintain good feeling with other Hellenes; and, indeed, we have reason to fear that the offering of spoils taken from kinsmen may be a pollution unless commanded by the god himself?"

"Very true."

"Again, as to the devastation of Hellenic territory or the burning of houses, what is to be the practice?"

"May I have the pleasure," he said, "of hearing your opinion?"

"Both should be forbidden, in my judgment; I would take the annual produce and no more. Shall I tell you why?"

"Pray do."

"Why, you see, there is a difference in the names 'discord' and 'war,' and I imagine that there is also a difference in their natures; the one is expressive of what is internal and domestic, the other of what is external and foreign; and the first of the two is termed discord, and only the second, war."
refashioned:
"Sparing them is infinitely better."

"Then no Greek should be held as a slave by our State. They'll follow this rule themselves and urge other Greeks to do the same."

"Certainly. This way Greeks will unite against barbarians and stop attacking each other."

"Now regarding the fallen in battle: should conquerors take anything beyond the enemy's armor? Doesn't stripping the dead provide cowards with an excuse to avoid fighting? They lurk around corpses pretending to do their duty, and many armies have been destroyed by this love of plunder."

"Very true."

"Isn't there something petty and greedy about robbing a corpse? And something contemptible about treating a dead body as the enemy when the actual enemy has fled, leaving only his equipment behind? It's like a dog that can't reach its attacker, so it snarls at the stones thrown at it instead."

"Very much like a dog."

"Then we must not strip the dead or prevent their burial."

"Absolutely not."

"Nor should we dedicate captured arms at temples -- especially Greek arms, if we want to maintain goodwill with other Greeks. Offering spoils taken from kinsmen may actually be a pollution, unless the god himself commands it."

"Very true."

"And what about devastating Greek territory or burning houses?"

"I'd like to hear your view."

"Both should be forbidden. Take only the annual harvest, nothing more. Let me explain why."

"Please do."

"There's a meaningful distinction between the words 'discord' and 'war.' Discord is internal and domestic; war is external and foreign. The first is properly called discord; only the second deserves the name war."
elegant: Greeks must not enslave, strip, or desecrate fellow Greeks' dead. Captured arms should not be dedicated at temples. Greek territory should not be devastated or houses burned -- only the annual harvest may be taken. The key distinction: conflict between Greeks is 'discord' (internal, among kin), while only conflict with barbarians is truly 'war' (external, against foreigners).
summary: Socrates establishes rules of engagement among Greeks: no enslavement, no stripping the dead, no temple dedications of Greek spoils, no devastation of territory. Only the annual harvest may be taken. The philosophical basis is that Greek-on-Greek conflict is 'discord' (domestic), not 'war' (foreign) -- Greeks are kin, not enemies.
distilled: Greek-on-Greek conflict is 'discord' among kin, not 'war' against foreigners -- requiring restraint in slavery, plunder, and destruction.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The discord/war (stasis/polemos) distinction is politically important: stasis was the Greek term for civil strife, the most feared form of internal conflict -- The prohibition on stripping the dead reflects Greek religious customs; improper treatment of the dead was considered sacrilege (cf. Sophocles' Antigone) -- The dog-and-stones simile is vivid Socratic imagery -- 'Pollution' (miasma) from kinsman-spoils reflects Greek religious anxiety about blood-guilt -- These rules of war anticipate modern laws of armed conflict and the distinction between interstate war and civil conflict

narrative:
"That is a very proper distinction," he replied.

"And may I not observe with equal propriety that the Hellenic race is all united together by ties of blood and friendship, and alien and strange to the barbarians?"

"Very good," he said.

"And therefore when Hellenes fight with barbarians and barbarians with Hellenes, they will be described by us as being at war when they fight, and by nature enemies, and this kind of antagonism should be called war; but when Hellenes fight with one another we shall say that Hellas is then in a state of disorder and discord, they being by nature friends; and such enmity is to be called discord."
refashioned:
"That's an important distinction," he replied.

"And can we not say with equal justice that the entire Greek race is united by ties of blood and friendship, while remaining alien and foreign to barbarians?"

"Indeed."

"So when Greeks fight barbarians and barbarians fight Greeks, we'll call that war -- they are enemies by nature, and the conflict deserves the name 'war.' But when Greeks fight each other, we'll say Greece is in a state of disorder and discord. Greeks are friends by nature, and their conflicts should be called discord, not war."
elegant: Greeks are united by blood and naturally friends; barbarians are naturally foreign. Greek-barbarian conflict is 'war' between natural enemies; Greek-Greek conflict is 'discord' between natural friends.
summary: Socrates distinguishes between war (natural conflict with foreign barbarians) and discord (unnatural conflict among kinship-bound Greeks). Greeks are united by blood; their internal conflicts are disorders within a family, not wars between enemies.
distilled: Greek-barbarian conflict is war between natural enemies; Greek-Greek conflict is discord among natural friends.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The Greek/barbarian dichotomy was fundamental to classical Greek identity -- 'By nature enemies' and 'by nature friends' (physei) appeals to natural law rather than convention -- This pan-Hellenic vision influenced Alexander the Great's propaganda, though Alexander complicated it by embracing Persian culture -- Aristotle similarly distinguished between Greeks and barbarians but drew different political conclusions (Politics I.2) -- The friendship/enmity framework connects to the Republic's broader theme of justice as harmony and injustice as discord

narrative:
"I agree."

"Consider then," I said, "when that which we have acknowledged to be discord occurs, and a city is divided, if both parties destroy the lands and burn the houses of one another, how wicked does the strife appear! No true lover of his country would bring himself to tear in pieces his own nurse and mother: There might be reason in the conqueror depriving the conquered of their harvest, but still they would have the idea of peace in their hearts and would not mean to go on fighting for ever."
refashioned: "Consider this," I said. "When discord breaks out and a city is divided, if both sides destroy each other's lands and burn each other's houses, how terrible that strife appears! No true patriot would bring himself to tear apart his own motherland -- the nurse and mother that raised him. It might be reasonable for the victors to seize the defeated side's harvest, but even so, they should hold peace in their hearts and not intend to fight forever."
elegant: In civil discord, destroying lands and burning houses is unconscionable -- no patriot tears apart his own motherland. Victors may take the harvest, but must keep peace in their hearts and aim for reconciliation, not perpetual conflict.
summary: Socrates argues that in civil discord, destroying property is unconscionable because both sides share the same motherland. Victors may seize the harvest but must aim for reconciliation, not perpetual conflict.
distilled: Civil conflict must aim at reconciliation, not destruction -- no patriot tears apart the motherland that nursed him.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Nurse and mother' (trophos kai meter) personifies the homeland as a nurturing parent -- This connects to the autochthony myth (citizens born from the earth) mentioned in Book III's 'noble lie' -- The restraint in civil conflict anticipates modern just war theory's distinction between jus ad bellum and jus in bello -- The insistence on eventual reconciliation differentiates Plato's view from theories of total war -- Greek city-states frequently violated these principles, as the devastation of Attica during the Peloponnesian War demonstrated

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is a better temper than the other."

"And will not the city, which you are founding, be an Hellenic city?"

"It ought to be," he replied.

"Then will not the citizens be good and civilized?"

"Yes, very civilized."

"And will they not be lovers of Hellas, and think of Hellas as their own land, and share in the common temples?"

"Most certainly."

"And any difference which arises among them will be regarded by them as discord only -- a quarrel among friends, which is not to be called a war?"

"Certainly not."

"Then they will quarrel as those who intend some day to be reconciled?"

"Certainly."

"They will use friendly correction, but will not enslave or destroy their opponents; they will be correctors, not enemies?"
refashioned:
"That's a far better attitude than the alternative."

"And the city we're founding will be a Greek city?"

"It should be."

"Its citizens will be good and civilized?"

"Very much so."

"They'll love Greece, consider it their own land, and share in the common temples?"

"Absolutely."

"Any conflict with other Greeks will be regarded as discord -- a quarrel among friends, not a war?"

"Certainly not a war."

"So they'll quarrel as people who intend to reconcile eventually?"

"Yes."

"They'll use friendly correction rather than enslavement or destruction -- acting as correctors, not enemies?"
elegant: The ideal Greek city will treat all conflicts with fellow Greeks as discord among friends, not war against enemies. They will aim at reconciliation and friendly correction, never enslavement or destruction.
summary: The ideal city, as a Greek city, will regard all conflicts with fellow Greeks as discord among friends rather than war against enemies, practicing correction rather than destruction and always aiming for eventual reconciliation.
distilled: The ideal city treats Greek-on-Greek conflict as a correctable family quarrel, never resorting to enslavement or destruction.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- 'Common temples' (koina hiera) refers to pan-Hellenic sanctuaries like Delphi and Olympia -- 'Correctors, not enemies' (nouthetountes, ou polemountes) distinguishes reformative from punitive approaches -- This vision of restrained warfare was not typical of Greek practice; the Peloponnesian War (431-404 BCE) saw extreme devastation -- The passage presents an idealized vision of pan-Hellenic relations that contrasts with historical reality -- The emphasis on reconciliation connects to justice as harmony, the Republic's central theme

narrative:
"Just so."

"And as they are Hellenes themselves they will not devastate Hellas, nor will they burn houses, nor even suppose that the whole population of a city -- men, women, and children -- are equally their enemies, for they know that the guilt of war is always confined to a few persons and that the many are their friends. And for all these reasons they will be unwilling to waste their lands and raze their houses; their enmity to them will only last until the many innocent sufferers have compelled the guilty few to give satisfaction?"
refashioned: "As fellow Greeks, they won't devastate Greek lands or burn houses. They won't assume that an entire city's population -- men, women, and children -- are all equally their enemies, because they understand that the guilt for conflict always belongs to a few people, while the majority are their friends. For all these reasons, they'll refuse to destroy lands or raze houses. Their hostility will persist only until the many innocent people compel the guilty few to make amends."
elegant: Greek warriors won't devastate Greek lands because guilt belongs to the few, not the many. The population -- men, women, and children -- are friends, not enemies. Hostility lasts only until the innocent majority forces the guilty few to make restitution.
summary: Socrates argues that since guilt for conflict belongs to a few leaders while the general population remains friends, Greek warriors should never devastate lands or destroy houses. Hostility should end when the innocent majority forces the guilty few to make amends.
distilled: War guilt belongs to a few leaders, not entire populations -- so Greek warriors must spare the innocent majority and their lands.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The distinction between guilty leaders and innocent populations is remarkably modern, anticipating international humanitarian law's principle of distinction -- 'The guilt of war is always confined to a few persons' prefigures modern concepts of war crimes responsibility -- This stands in sharp contrast to ancient practices of collective punishment (e.g., Athens' destruction of Melos in 416 BCE, recounted by Thucydides) -- The principle that hostility should end with reparation rather than destruction anticipates restorative justice concepts

narrative:
"I agree," he said, "that our citizens should thus deal with their Hellenic enemies; and with barbarians as the Hellenes now deal with one another."

"Then let us enact this law also for our guardians: -- that they are neither to devastate the lands of Hellenes nor to burn their houses."

"Agreed; and we may agree also in thinking that these, like all our previous enactments, are very good."
refashioned:
"I agree," he said. "Our citizens should treat Greek adversaries with restraint, while treating barbarians the way Greeks currently treat each other."

"Then let this be law for our guardians: they shall neither devastate Greek lands nor burn Greek houses."

"Agreed. And we can also agree that these laws, like all our previous ones, are excellent."
elegant: Restraint is reserved for Greek adversaries; barbarians receive the treatment Greeks currently inflict on each other. The law: no devastating Greek lands, no burning Greek houses.
summary: Socrates codifies the rule: guardians will treat Greek adversaries with restraint while treating barbarians as Greeks currently treat each other. Greek lands and houses are never to be devastated.
distilled: The law is enacted: restraint toward Greek enemies, but barbarians may be treated as Greeks currently treat each other.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The double standard (restraint for Greeks, harshness for barbarians) reveals the limits of Plato's humanism -- 'As the Hellenes now deal with one another' implies current Greek-on-Greek warfare is excessively brutal -- This passage completes the rules of engagement section -- The distinction between Greek and barbarian treatment would later be challenged by Stoic cosmopolitanism and Christian universalism -- Alexander the Great's policies of cultural fusion challenged this Greek/barbarian divide in practice

narrative: "But still I must say, Socrates, that if you are allowed to go on in this way you will entirely forget the other question which at the commencement of this discussion you thrust aside: -- Is such an order of things possible, and how, if at all? For I am quite ready to acknowledge that the plan which you propose, if only feasible, would do all sorts of good to the State. I will add, what you have omitted, that your citizens will be the bravest of warriors, and will never leave their ranks, for they will all know one another, and each will call the other father, brother, son; and if you suppose the women to join their armies, whether in the same rank or in the rear, either as a terror to the enemy, or as auxiliaries in case of need, I know that they will then be absolutely invincible; and there are many domestic advantages which might also be mentioned and which I also fully acknowledge: but, as I admit all these advantages and as many more as you please, if only this State of yours were to come into existence, we need say no more about them; assuming then the existence of the State, let us now turn to the question of possibility and ways and means -- the rest may be left."
refashioned:
"But Socrates, if you keep going this way, you'll completely forget the question you pushed aside at the start: Is this order of things actually possible, and if so, how? I'm fully prepared to acknowledge that your plan, if feasible, would do enormous good for the State. I'll even add what you've omitted: your citizens will be the bravest warriors, never abandoning their positions, since they'll all know each other as fathers, brothers, and sons. If women join the armies -- whether fighting alongside the men or supporting from the rear, as a terror to the enemy or as auxiliaries -- they'll be absolutely invincible. There are countless domestic advantages too, which I fully acknowledge.

But since I concede all these benefits and more, we needn't discuss them further. Assuming the State can exist, let's finally address the real question: Is it possible, and by what means?"
elegant: Glaucon concedes all the benefits -- military invincibility through family bonds, women in armies, domestic harmony -- but demands Socrates finally address the deferred question: Is this State actually possible, and how?
summary: Glaucon concedes every claimed benefit of the ideal State -- military bravery through family bonds, women warriors, domestic harmony -- but insists Socrates must finally address the question he has been avoiding: is this State actually possible, and by what means?
distilled: Glaucon concedes all benefits but demands the deferred answer: is this ideal State actually possible?
notes: Speakers: Glaucon -- Glaucon's comprehensive concession clears the way for the third and greatest wave -- The mention of women in armies 'as a terror to the enemy' suggests psychological warfare -- 'Absolutely invincible' (amachoi) is a strong claim about communal military superiority -- This passage marks the pivotal transition from the second wave (community of families) to the third wave (philosopher-kings) -- The question of possibility is the most important question in the Republic and receives the most radical answer

narrative: "If I loiter for a moment, you instantly make a raid upon me," I said, "and have no mercy; I have hardly escaped the first and second waves, and you seem not to be aware that you are now bringing upon me the third, which is the greatest and heaviest. When you have seen and heard the third wave, I think you will be more considerate and will acknowledge that some fear and hesitation was natural respecting a proposal so extraordinary as that which I have now to state and investigate."
refashioned: "The moment I pause, you raid me without mercy," I said. "I've barely survived the first and second waves, and you don't seem to realize you're now bringing the third down upon me -- the greatest and most overwhelming of all. When you've seen and heard this third wave, I think you'll be more sympathetic and acknowledge that some fear and hesitation was perfectly natural regarding a proposal as extraordinary as the one I'm about to present."
elegant: Socrates warns that the third wave -- the greatest and heaviest -- is about to break. When they hear it, they'll understand his fear and hesitation about this most extraordinary proposal.
summary: Socrates warns that after surviving the first wave (women's equality) and the second (communal families), the third and greatest wave is about to crash upon them -- a proposal so extraordinary that his fear and hesitation will finally be understood.
distilled: The third wave -- the greatest and most extraordinary proposal -- is about to break, and Socrates warns it will justify all his prior hesitation.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- The 'three waves' metaphor reaches its climax: (1) women's equality, (2) community of families, (3) philosopher-kings -- In Greek maritime culture, the 'third wave' was proverbially the largest and most dangerous -- Socrates' dramatic buildup heightens the impact of the philosopher-king declaration -- The military metaphor of 'raiding' continues the warfare imagery of the preceding section -- This is one of the great dramatic moments in the Republic

narrative:
"The more appeals of this sort which you make," he said, "the more determined are we that you shall tell us how such a State is possible: speak out and at once."

"Let me begin by reminding you that we found our way hither in the search after justice and injustice."

"True," he replied; "but what of that?"

"I was only going to ask whether, if we have discovered them, we are to require that the just man should in nothing fail of absolute justice; or may we be satisfied with an approximation, and the attainment in him of a higher degree of justice than is to be found in other men?"
refashioned:
"The more you stall, the more determined we are that you tell us how this State is possible. Speak -- now."

"Let me begin by reminding you that our entire journey has been a search for justice and injustice."

"True. But what's your point?"

"Just this: now that we've identified what justice is, must we demand that the just person achieve absolute, perfect justice in every respect? Or can we be satisfied with an approximation -- someone who attains a higher degree of justice than other people?"
elegant: Before addressing possibility, Socrates reframes the question: must justice be perfectly achieved, or is approximation sufficient? Since their inquiry aimed at understanding justice and injustice, perhaps an approximation of the ideal State is all that can be expected.
summary: Pressed to explain how the ideal State is possible, Socrates first asks whether they require perfect justice or can accept approximation. Since their inquiry was about understanding justice, perhaps a higher degree of justice -- not absolute perfection -- is a sufficient standard.
distilled: Before addressing possibility, Socrates asks: must justice be perfect, or is approximation of the ideal sufficient?
notes: Speakers: Glaucon and Socrates -- This methodological reframing is crucial: it lowers the bar from 'exactly realizable' to 'approximately achievable' -- The reminder that the inquiry began with justice (Book I) reconnects to the Republic's primary question -- The concept of approximation to an ideal anticipates the Forms theory developed in Books VI-VII -- This move also anticipates the painter analogy in 5.71 -- The question 'what of that?' shows Glaucon's impatience with what seems like another delay tactic

narrative:
"The approximation will be enough."

"We are enquiring into the nature of absolute justice and into the character of the perfectly just, and into injustice and the perfectly unjust, that we might have an ideal. We were to look at these in order that we might judge of our own happiness and unhappiness according to the standard which they exhibited and the degree in which we resembled them, but not with any view of showing that they could exist in fact."
refashioned:
"Approximation will be enough."

"We've been investigating the nature of absolute justice and the perfectly just person, alongside absolute injustice and the perfectly unjust, in order to have an ideal standard. We examine these ideals so we can measure our own happiness and unhappiness by how closely we resemble them -- not with the expectation that they could actually exist in reality."
elegant: Ideals of perfect justice and injustice serve as measuring standards for real life. They reveal how closely we approximate the ideal, not whether the ideal can literally exist. Approximation is sufficient.
summary: Socrates clarifies that their investigation of perfect justice was never meant to prove such perfection could exist. The ideal serves as a standard by which to measure real happiness and virtue -- approximation to the ideal is the goal, not its literal realization.
distilled: The ideal of perfect justice serves as a standard for measuring real life, not as a blueprint that must be literally realized.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage introduces the crucial distinction between ideal and reality, Forms and their instantiations -- 'That we might have an ideal' (paradeigma) uses the word that becomes 'paradigm' -- This philosophical move anticipates the Theory of Forms developed in Books VI-VII -- The relationship between ideal and approximation is central to Platonic metaphysics -- This reframing allows Socrates to defend the ideal State as a valuable model even if it can never be perfectly realized -- Compare Kant's later distinction between regulative and constitutive ideals

narrative:
"True," he said.

"Would a painter be any the worse because, after having delineated with consummate art an ideal of a perfectly beautiful man, he was unable to show that any such man could ever have existed?"

"He would be none the worse."

"Well, and were we not creating an ideal of a perfect State?"

"To be sure."

"And is our theory a worse theory because we are unable to prove the possibility of a city being ordered in the manner described?"

"Surely not," he replied.

"That is the truth," I said. "But if, at your request, I am to try and show how and under what conditions the possibility is highest, I must ask you, having this in view, to repeat your former admissions."

"What admissions?"

"I want to know whether ideals are ever fully realised in language? Does not the word express more than the fact, and must not the actual, whatever a man may think, always, in the nature of things, fall short of the truth? What do you say?"

"I agree."

"Then you must not insist on my proving that the actual State will in every respect coincide with the ideal: if we are only able to discover how a city may be governed nearly as we proposed, you will admit that we have discovered the possibility which you demand; and will be contented. I am sure that I should be contented -- will not you?"

"Yes, I will."

"Let me next endeavour to show what is that fault in States which is the cause of their present maladministration, and what is the least change which will enable a State to pass into the truer form; and let the change, if possible, be of one thing only, or if not, of two; at any rate, let the changes be as few and slight as possible."

"Certainly," he replied.

"I think," I said, "that there might be a reform of the State if only one change were made, which is not a slight or easy though still a possible one."

"What is it?" he said.

"Now then," I said, "I go to meet that which I liken to the greatest of the waves; yet shall the word be spoken, even though the wave break and drown me in laughter and dishonour; and do you mark my words."
refashioned:
"Would a painter be any worse at his art because, after creating an ideal portrait of a perfectly beautiful person, he couldn't prove such a person ever existed?"

"Not at all."

"And weren't we creating an ideal of a perfect State?"

"Yes."

"Is our theory any worse because we can't prove a city could be organized exactly as we've described?"

"Surely not."

"Exactly. But if you want me to try to show how and under what conditions this comes closest to possibility, I need you to accept something first."

"What?"

"Are ideals ever perfectly captured even in words? Doesn't language always express more than facts can deliver? Must not reality, by its very nature, always fall short of the ideal?"

"I agree."

"Then don't insist that I prove the actual State will match the ideal in every detail. If we can discover how a city might be governed approximately as we've proposed, you'll have to admit we've demonstrated the possibility you demanded. I'd be satisfied with that -- wouldn't you?"

"Yes."

"Then let me try to identify the single fault in existing States that causes their maladministration, and the smallest change that would bring a State closer to the true form. Let the change be one thing only, if possible -- or at most two -- as few and as small as possible."

"Certainly."

"I believe one single change could reform the State. It's neither slight nor easy, but it is possible."

"What is it?"

"Now I go to meet what I've compared to the greatest of waves. The word shall be spoken, even if the wave breaks over me and drowns me in laughter and dishonor. Mark my words."
elegant: A painter is not diminished because his ideal portrait has no real-world match. Similarly, the ideal State's theory is not weakened by imperfect realization -- reality always falls short of ideals. Socrates needs only show approximate possibility. He identifies one single change that could reform existing States, and prepares to face the 'greatest wave' -- the proposal that may drown him in ridicule.
summary: Socrates uses the painter analogy to argue that ideals need not be perfectly realizable to be valuable -- reality always falls short of truth. He then narrows the question: what single change could bring existing States closest to the ideal? He prepares dramatically to face the 'greatest wave,' expecting ridicule and dishonor.
distilled: Ideals need not be perfectly realized to be valuable; one single change could reform States -- and Socrates braces for the ridicule his answer will bring.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The painter analogy is central to Plato's epistemology: the artist creates idealized representations, just as the philosopher contemplates ideal Forms -- 'Does not the word express more than the fact' raises the relationship between language, thought, and reality -- 'The least change' (elachiste metabole) sets up the philosopher-king proposal as a single necessary and sufficient condition -- The wave metaphor reaches its dramatic peak -- 'Drown me in laughter and dishonor' shows Socrates' awareness of how radical his next claim will be -- This passage is the most carefully constructed dramatic buildup in the Republic

narrative:
"Proceed."

I said: "Until philosophers are kings, or the kings and princes of this world have the spirit and power of philosophy, and political greatness and wisdom meet in one, and those commoner natures who pursue either to the exclusion of the other are compelled to stand aside, cities will never have rest from their evils, -- nor the human race, as I believe, -- and then only will this our State have a possibility of life and behold the light of day. Such was the thought, my dear Glaucon, which I would fain have uttered if it had not seemed too extravagant; for to be convinced that in no other State can there be happiness private or public is indeed a hard thing."
refashioned:
"Go on."

"Until philosophers become kings, or the kings and rulers of this world acquire the spirit and power of genuine philosophy -- until political authority and philosophical wisdom are united in the same person, and those ordinary people who pursue one while excluding the other are compelled to step aside -- cities will never find rest from their troubles. Nor, I believe, will the human race itself. Only then will our ideal State have any chance of coming into existence and seeing the light of day.

This, dear Glaucon, is the thought I've been reluctant to speak, because it seemed too outrageous. It is indeed a hard truth to accept: that no State can achieve happiness, public or private, by any other means."
elegant: Until philosophers become kings or kings become philosophers -- until political power and philosophical wisdom unite in one person -- cities will never escape their evils, nor will the human race. Only this fusion gives the ideal State any possibility of existence.
summary: Socrates delivers the Republic's most famous thesis: cities will never be free from evil until philosophers become kings or kings become philosophers. Political power and wisdom must unite in the same person. Only this single change makes the ideal State possible.
distilled: Until philosophers are kings or kings are philosophers, cities and humanity will never escape their evils.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This is the most famous passage in the Republic, perhaps in all of Plato -- The philosopher-king thesis is the 'third wave' and the Republic's central political proposal -- 'Those commoner natures who pursue either to the exclusion of the other' criticizes both unworldly intellectuals and unphilosophical politicians -- 'Nor the human race' extends the claim beyond city-states to all humanity -- Plato may have attempted to realize this ideal with his advisory role to Dionysius II of Syracuse (367-366 BCE), which failed dramatically -- The Seventh Letter discusses Plato's Syracuse experience -- This idea has influenced political philosophy from the Islamic falsafa tradition (Al-Farabi's 'Virtuous City') to modern technocratic ideals

narrative: "Socrates, what do you mean? I would have you consider that the word which you have uttered is one at which numerous persons, and very respectable persons too, in a figure pulling off their coats all in a moment, and seizing any weapon that comes to hand, will run at you might and main, before you know where you are, intending to do heaven knows what; and if you don't prepare an answer, and put yourself in motion, you will be 'pared by their fine wits,' and no mistake."
refashioned: "Socrates, do you realize what you've just said? Consider that a great many people -- and very respectable ones too -- will figuratively rip off their coats, grab whatever weapons are at hand, and charge at you full force before you know what's happening. Heaven knows what they intend to do to you. If you don't prepare your defense and start moving, you'll be torn to shreds by their sharp wits -- make no mistake."
elegant: Glaucon warns Socrates that his philosopher-king thesis will provoke an intellectual assault: respectable people will arm themselves with arguments and charge at him. He must prepare his defense immediately.
summary: Glaucon warns Socrates that the philosopher-king claim will provoke a violent intellectual reaction from numerous respectable people who will attack him with every argument at their disposal unless he prepares his defense immediately.
distilled: Glaucon warns that the philosopher-king thesis will provoke a furious intellectual attack from many respectable people.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon -- The vivid imagery of people ripping off coats and grabbing weapons dramatizes the controversy of the philosopher-king idea -- 'Very respectable persons' suggests both politicians and conventional philosophers -- 'Pared by their fine wits' (a Jowett translation of a wrestling metaphor) implies being intellectually pinned down -- The warning reflects genuine historical resistance to the idea that intellectuals should rule -- This dramatic moment transitions from the thesis statement to its defense, which occupies the rest of Book V and extends into Books VI-VII

narrative:
"You got me into the scrape," I said.

"And I was quite right; however, I will do all I can to get you out of it; but I can only give you good-will and good advice, and, perhaps, I may be able to fit answers to your questions better than another -- that is all. And now, having such an auxiliary, you must do your best to show the unbelievers that you are right."
refashioned:
"You're the one who got me into this mess," I said.

"And rightly so. But I'll do everything I can to help you through it. All I can offer is goodwill, good advice, and perhaps better-fitting answers to your questions than someone else might. With this support behind you, you must now do your best to convince the skeptics that you're right."
elegant: Glaucon accepts responsibility for forcing the issue but offers to support Socrates with goodwill and sharp questioning. Now Socrates must defend the philosopher-king thesis against skeptics.
summary: Socrates blames Glaucon for forcing him into this difficult position. Glaucon accepts responsibility and pledges support through good questioning, but insists Socrates must now defend the philosopher-king thesis to the unconvinced.
distilled: Glaucon pledges to support Socrates in defending the philosopher-king thesis he forced into the open.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- Glaucon's role as 'auxiliary' (epikouros) uses the Republic's own terminology for the warrior class -- The exchange shows the collaborative nature of dialectic: Glaucon pushes Socrates but also supports him -- 'Fit answers to your questions' suggests that the right questions can elicit the right answers, reflecting Socratic maieutics (intellectual midwifery) -- The transition from thesis to defense structures the remainder of Book V and the beginning of Book VI

narrative: "I ought to try," I said, "since you offer me such invaluable assistance. And I think that, if there is to be a chance of our escaping, we must explain to them whom we mean when we say that philosophers are to rule in the State; then we shall be able to defend ourselves: There will be discovered to be some natures who ought to study philosophy and to be leaders in the State; and others who are not born to be philosophers, and are meant to be followers rather than leaders."
refashioned: "I should try," I said, "given your invaluable support. And I think our best chance of surviving this argument is to explain exactly whom we mean by 'philosophers' when we say they should rule. Once we do, we can mount a proper defense. We'll discover that some natures are suited to philosophy and leadership, while others are born to be followers rather than leaders."
elegant: The defense begins with a crucial clarification: who exactly are these 'philosophers' who should rule? Some natures are suited to philosophy and leadership; others are meant to follow.
summary: Socrates identifies the key to defending the philosopher-king thesis: they must define exactly who counts as a 'philosopher.' Some natures are suited to philosophy and leadership, while others are not -- and distinguishing the two is essential.
distilled: The philosopher-king defense begins by defining who qualifies as a 'philosopher' -- distinguishing natural leaders from natural followers.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This passage launches the epistemological investigation that dominates the rest of Book V -- The distinction between philosophical and non-philosophical natures connects to the guardian selection process in Books II-III -- 'Born to be philosophers' suggests natural aptitude, consistent with the earlier arguments about nature and education -- The definition of the philosopher will culminate in the famous distinction between knowledge and opinion (5.80-5.88) -- This investigation continues into Books VI (the Good, the Sun analogy, the Divided Line) and VII (the Cave allegory)

narrative:
"Then now for a definition," he said.

"Follow me," I said, "and I hope that I may in some way or other be able to give you a satisfactory explanation."

"Proceed."

"I dare say that you remember, and therefore I need not remind you, that a lover, if he is worthy of the name, ought to show his love, not to some one part of that which he loves, but to the whole."

"I really do not understand, and therefore beg of you to assist my memory."
refashioned:
"Let's have a definition, then," he said.

"Follow my reasoning," I said, "and I hope to give you a satisfactory explanation."

"Go ahead."

"You surely remember -- so I needn't remind you -- that a true lover ought to love the whole of what he loves, not just one part of it."

"Actually, I don't quite follow. Please explain."
elegant: Socrates begins defining the philosopher with a principle about love: a true lover loves the whole of what he loves, not just a part. Glaucon asks for clarification.
summary: Socrates begins defining the philosopher by establishing a principle about love: a genuine lover must love the entirety of what he loves, not merely selected parts. Glaucon asks him to elaborate.
distilled: A true lover loves the whole of what he loves -- the starting point for defining the philosopher.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The philosopher as 'lover of wisdom' (philosophos = philo + sophia) is being taken literally: what does it mean to love something? -- The principle that a true lover loves the whole connects to the definition of the philosopher as lover of all wisdom, not just parts -- Glaucon's request for clarification (or feigned ignorance) is a dialectical device that allows Socrates to develop the argument through concrete examples -- This approach through eros connects to the Symposium's exploration of love as philosophical motivation

narrative: "Another person," I said, "might fairly reply as you do; but a man of pleasure like yourself ought to know that all who are in the flower of youth do somehow or other raise a pang or emotion in a lover's breast, and are thought by him to be worthy of his affectionate regards. Is not this a way which you have with the fair: one has a snub nose, and you praise his charming face; the hook-nose of another has, you say, a royal look; while he who is neither snub nor hooked has the grace of regularity: the dark visage is manly, the fair are children of the gods; and as to the sweet 'honey pale,' as they are called, what is the very name but the invention of a lover who talks in diminutives, and is not adverse to paleness if appearing on the cheek of youth? In a word, there is no excuse which you will not make, and nothing which you will not say, in order not to lose a single flower that blooms in the spring-time of youth."
refashioned:
"Someone else might fairly claim not to understand," I said, "but a man of your romantic experience should know this well. Every person in the flower of youth stirs some feeling in a lover's heart and is deemed worthy of affection. Isn't this how you treat the beautiful? One has a snub nose -- you praise his charming face. Another has a hooked nose -- you say it has a regal quality. The one with a straight nose has the grace of perfect proportion. A dark complexion is called manly; a fair one marks children of the gods. And that 'honey-pale' complexion? The very term was invented by a lover speaking in endearments, willing to find beauty in pallor as long as it appears on a youthful cheek.

In short, there's no excuse you won't make and nothing you won't say to avoid losing a single flower from youth's springtime garden."
elegant: Socrates teases Glaucon: a true lover of beauty finds something to praise in every young person -- snub noses are charming, hooked noses are regal, dark skin is manly, fair skin is divine. A real lover makes every excuse to appreciate every flower of youth, refusing to reject any.
summary: Socrates playfully illustrates his point through Glaucon's romantic life: a true lover finds beauty in every young person, praising snub noses, hooked noses, dark skin, and fair skin alike. A genuine lover embraces the whole, never rejecting a single flower of youth.
distilled: A true lover of beauty finds something to praise in every youth -- snub, hooked, dark, or fair -- refusing to reject any.
notes: Speakers: Socrates -- This passage is one of the Republic's most vivid and humorous -- Socrates teases Glaucon about his reputation as a lover of beautiful youth, established earlier in the dialogue -- The examples of nose types and skin colors reflect Greek aesthetic categories -- 'Honey-pale' (melichros) is a Greek diminutive term of endearment -- The argument by analogy: just as a true lover embraces all beauty, a true philosopher embraces all wisdom -- The erotic language connects to the Symposium and Phaedrus, where eros drives philosophical ascent -- This passage humanizes the abstract philosophical argument with concrete, relatable humor

narrative:
"If you make me an authority in matters of love, for the sake of the argument, I assent."

"And what do you say of lovers of wine? Do you not see them doing the same? They are glad of any pretext of drinking any wine."

"Very good."

"And the same is true of ambitious men; if they cannot command an army, they are willing to command a file; and if they cannot be honoured by really great and important persons, they are glad to be honoured by lesser and meaner people, but honour of some kind they must have."

"Exactly."

"Once more let me ask: Does he who desires any class of goods, desire the whole class or a part only?"

"The whole."

"And may we not say of the philosopher that he is a lover, not of a part of wisdom only, but of the whole?"

"Yes, of the whole."

"And he who dislikes learning, especially in youth, when he has no power of judging what is good and what is not, such an one we maintain not to be a philosopher or a lover of knowledge, just as he who refuses his food is not hungry, and may be said to have a bad appetite and not a good one?"

"Very true," he said.

"Whereas he who has a taste for every sort of knowledge and who is curious to learn and is never satisfied, may be justly termed a philosopher? Am I not right?"
refashioned:
"If you insist on making me an expert on love for argument's sake, I'll go along with it."

"And what about wine lovers? Don't they do the same thing? They'll take any excuse to drink any wine."

"True enough."

"The same goes for ambitious people. If they can't command an army, they'll settle for commanding a squad. If they can't be honored by truly great people, they'll accept honor from lesser ones. But they must have honor of some kind."

"Exactly."

"So let me ask again: does someone who desires a class of things desire the whole class, or only part?"

"The whole."

"Then can we say the philosopher is a lover of all wisdom, not just part of it?"

"Yes, all of it."

"And someone who dislikes learning -- especially in youth, when they can't yet judge what's valuable and what isn't -- is no philosopher or lover of knowledge. Just as someone who refuses food isn't hungry and can be said to have a poor appetite rather than a good one."

"Very true."

"But someone who has a taste for every kind of knowledge, who is curious to learn and never satisfied -- that person may rightly be called a philosopher?"
elegant: As wine lovers drink any wine and ambitious people accept any honor, the true philosopher loves all wisdom, not just part. Someone who dislikes learning has a bad intellectual appetite. The philosopher is defined by insatiable, universal curiosity.
summary: Socrates extends his analogy: wine lovers drink any wine, ambitious people accept any honor, and true philosophers love all wisdom. The philosopher is defined by universal curiosity and an insatiable appetite for learning, while those who dislike learning have no philosophical nature.
distilled: The philosopher is defined by universal, insatiable love of all wisdom -- just as the lover loves all beauty and the wine-lover drinks all wine.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The parallel between lovers, wine-enthusiasts, ambitious people, and philosophers illustrates the principle: genuine desire embraces the whole class, not a part -- The food/appetite metaphor for learning connects to the soul's appetitive part (Book IV) -- 'Never satisfied' (aplestia) in this context is a virtue, not a vice -- This definition will be refined: not all curious people are philosophers (as Glaucon objects in 5.79) -- The distinction between genuine and false philosophy becomes crucial in Books VI-VII

narrative: Glaucon said: "If curiosity makes a philosopher, you will find many a strange being will have a title to the name. All the lovers of sights have a delight in learning, and must therefore be included. Musical amateurs, too, are a folk strangely out of place among philosophers, for they are the last persons in the world who would come to anything like a philosophical discussion, if they could help, while they run about at the Dionysiac festivals as if they had let out their ears to hear every chorus; whether the performance is in town or country -- that makes no difference -- they are there. Now are we to maintain that all these and any who have similar tastes, as well as the professors of quite minor arts, are philosophers?"
refashioned: Glaucon objected: "If mere curiosity makes someone a philosopher, you'll end up with a very strange group claiming the title. All the people who love spectacles take delight in learning -- should they be included? And music enthusiasts are an odd fit for philosophy. They're the last people who'd voluntarily attend a philosophical discussion, yet they chase every performance at the Dionysiac festivals as if they'd rented out their ears to every chorus. Town or country, it doesn't matter -- they're always there. Are we really going to call these people, and everyone with similar tastes, and practitioners of minor crafts, philosophers?"
elegant: Glaucon objects: if loving to learn makes one a philosopher, then spectacle-lovers and music festival enthusiasts -- who chase every performance but would never attend a philosophical discussion -- would qualify. Mere curiosity cannot be sufficient.
summary: Glaucon raises a sharp objection: if mere curiosity defines the philosopher, then sightseers, music festival enthusiasts, and hobbyists would all qualify. These people chase every spectacle but would never engage in actual philosophical discussion. The definition needs refinement.
distilled: Glaucon objects: mere curiosity is too broad a definition -- sightseers and music enthusiasts love learning but aren't philosophers.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon -- This is an excellent philosophical objection that forces Socrates to distinguish between lovers of spectacle (philotheamones) and lovers of wisdom (philosophoi) -- The Dionysiac festivals featured dramatic competitions (tragedies, comedies, dithyrambs) -- 'Let out their ears' is a vivid metaphor for indiscriminate consumption -- This objection drives the epistemological distinction between knowledge and opinion that concludes Book V -- The 'professors of quite minor arts' (technai) shows that mere technical skill also doesn't qualify as philosophy -- Glaucon's objection is genuinely incisive, not a straw man

narrative:
"Certainly not, I replied; they are only an imitation."

He said: "Who then are the true philosophers?"

"Those," I said, "who are lovers of the vision of truth."

"That is also good," he said; "but I should like to know what you mean?"

"To another," I replied, "I might have a difficulty in explaining; but I am sure that you will admit a proposition which I am about to make."

"What is the proposition?"

"That since beauty is the opposite of ugliness, they are two?"

"Certainly."

"And inasmuch as they are two, each of them is one?"

"True again."

"And of just and unjust, good and evil, and of every other class, the same remark holds: taken singly, each of them is one; but from the various combinations of them with actions and things and with one another, they are seen in all sorts of lights and appear many?"

"Very true."

"And this is the distinction which I draw between the sight-loving, art-loving, practical class and those of whom I am speaking, and who are alone worthy of the name of philosophers."

"How do you distinguish them?" he said.

"The lovers of sounds and sights," I replied, "are, as I conceive, fond of fine tones and colours and forms and all the artificial products that are made out of them, but their mind is incapable of seeing or loving absolute beauty."

"True," he replied.

"Few are they who are able to attain to the sight of this."

"Very true."

"And he who, having a sense of beautiful things has no sense of absolute beauty, or who, if another lead him to a knowledge of that beauty is unable to follow -- of such an one I ask, Is he awake or in a dream only? Reflect: is not the dreamer, sleeping or waking, one who likens dissimilar things, who puts the copy in the place of the real object?"

"I should certainly say that such an one was dreaming."

"But take the case of the other, who recognises the existence of absolute beauty and is able to distinguish the idea from the objects which participate in the idea, neither putting the objects in the place of the idea nor the idea in the place of the objects -- is he a dreamer, or is he awake?"
refashioned:
"Certainly not," I replied. "They're only imitations of philosophers."

"Then who are the true philosophers?"

"Those who love the vision of truth."

"Good -- but what do you mean by that?"

"With someone else, this might be hard to explain. But I'm confident you'll accept my starting proposition."

"What proposition?"

"Beauty is the opposite of ugliness -- so they are two distinct things?"

"Certainly."

"And being two, each is one?"

"True."

"The same applies to just and unjust, good and evil, and every other pair of opposites. Taken individually, each Form is one. But through their combinations with actions, objects, and each other, they appear in countless guises and seem to be many."

"Very true."

"This is the distinction I draw between the sight-loving, art-loving, practical crowd and those who alone deserve the name 'philosopher.'"

"How do you distinguish them?"

"The lovers of sights and sounds," I said, "are fond of beautiful colors, forms, tones, and all the products made from them. But their minds cannot see or love Beauty itself -- the absolute."

"True."

"Very few can attain the vision of absolute Beauty."

"Very true."

"Now consider: someone who appreciates beautiful things but has no awareness of Beauty itself -- or who, when guided toward it, cannot follow -- is that person awake or dreaming? Think about it: isn't a dreamer, whether sleeping or waking, someone who mistakes the copy for the original, confusing the likeness with the real thing?"

"I'd certainly say such a person is dreaming."

"But what about someone who recognizes the existence of absolute Beauty and can distinguish the Form from the particular things that participate in it -- never confusing the objects with the Form or the Form with the objects? Is that person dreaming, or awake?"
elegant: True philosophers love the vision of truth itself -- the Forms. Beauty, justice, and goodness are each one, though they appear as many through combination with actions and objects. Those who love beautiful things but cannot grasp Beauty itself are like dreamers who mistake copies for originals. Only those who distinguish Forms from their instances are truly awake.
summary: Socrates distinguishes true philosophers from mere lovers of spectacle. Each Form (Beauty, Justice, Good) is one, though it appears as many. Those who appreciate beautiful things but cannot grasp absolute Beauty are dreamers, confusing copies with originals. True philosophers recognize the Forms and distinguish them from their particular instantiations -- they alone are awake.
distilled: True philosophers see the one Form behind the many appearances -- distinguishing Beauty itself from beautiful things, awake while others dream.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This passage introduces the Theory of Forms (eidos/idea) explicitly in the Republic -- The one/many problem (how one Form manifests as many instances) is central to Platonic metaphysics -- The dreaming/waking distinction parallels the later Cave allegory (Book VII) -- 'Absolute beauty' (auto to kalon) is the Form of Beauty, distinct from beautiful objects -- 'Participate in the idea' (metechein) introduces the crucial concept of participation (methexis), which Plato interrogates in the Parmenides -- This passage marks the transition from political philosophy to metaphysics and epistemology -- The distinction between philosophers and sight-lovers maps onto the later distinction between knowledge and opinion

narrative:
"He is wide awake."

"And may we not say that the mind of the one who knows has knowledge, and that the mind of the other, who opines only, has opinion?"

"Certainly."

"But suppose that the latter should quarrel with us and dispute our statement, can we administer any soothing cordial or advice to him, without revealing to him that there is sad disorder in his wits?"

"We must certainly offer him some good advice," he replied.

"Come, then, and let us think of something to say to him. Shall we begin by assuring him that he is welcome to any knowledge which he may have, and that we are rejoiced at his having it? But we should like to ask him a question: Does he who has knowledge know something or nothing?"

"(You must answer for him.)"

"I answer that he knows something."

"Something that is or is not?"

"Something that is; for how can that which is not ever be known?"

"And are we assured, after looking at the matter from many points of view, that absolute being is or may be absolutely known, but that the utterly non-existent is utterly unknown?"

"Nothing can be more certain."

"Good. But if there be anything which is of such a nature as to be and not to be, that will have a place intermediate between pure being and the absolute negation of being?"

"Yes, between them."

"And, as knowledge corresponded to being and ignorance of necessity to not-being, for that intermediate between being and not-being there has to be discovered a corresponding intermediate between ignorance and knowledge, if there be such?"

"Certainly."

"Do we admit the existence of opinion?"

"Undoubtedly."

"As being the same with knowledge, or another faculty?"

"Another faculty."

"Then opinion and knowledge have to do with different kinds of matter corresponding to this difference of faculties?"

"Yes."

"And knowledge is relative to being and knows being. But before I proceed further I will make a division."

"What division?"

"I will begin by placing faculties in a class by themselves: they are powers in us, and in all other things, by which we do as we do. Sight and hearing, for example, I should call faculties. Have I clearly explained the class which I mean?"
refashioned:
"He is fully awake."

"Then can we say that the knower's mind possesses knowledge, while the opiner's mind possesses only opinion?"

"Certainly."

"But if the opiner disputes this, can we gently correct him without revealing how disordered his thinking is?"

"We should certainly try to offer good advice."

"Then let's think about what to say to him. We'll start by assuring him that we welcome whatever knowledge he has and are glad of it. But we'd like to ask a question: does someone who has knowledge know something, or nothing?"

"(Answer for him.)"

"He knows something."

"Something that exists, or something that doesn't?"

"Something that exists -- how could anyone know what doesn't exist?"

"And can we be certain, having examined this from every angle, that what fully exists can be fully known, while what absolutely does not exist is absolutely unknowable?"

"Nothing could be more certain."

"Good. But if something has a nature that is partly being and partly not-being, it would occupy an intermediate position between pure existence and pure non-existence?"

"Yes, between them."

"And since knowledge corresponds to being and ignorance to non-being, there must be some intermediate faculty between knowledge and ignorance that corresponds to this intermediate between being and non-being?"

"There must."

"Do we acknowledge the existence of opinion?"

"Of course."

"Is opinion the same as knowledge, or a different faculty?"

"A different faculty."

"Then opinion and knowledge, being different faculties, deal with different subject matters?"

"Yes."

"Knowledge is relative to being -- it knows what is. But before going further, let me make a distinction."

"What distinction?"

"I'll define 'faculties' as a class: they are powers in us and in all things by which we do what we do. Sight and hearing, for example, are faculties. Is the concept clear?"
elegant: Knowledge corresponds to being, ignorance to non-being. Since some things both are and are not (the sensible world), there must be an intermediate faculty between knowledge and ignorance -- this is opinion. Knowledge and opinion are distinct faculties with distinct objects. Socrates introduces 'faculties' as powers by which we operate.
summary: Socrates builds his epistemological framework: knowledge corresponds to being (what fully exists), ignorance to non-being (what doesn't exist). Since some things partake of both being and non-being, there must be an intermediate faculty -- opinion -- between knowledge and ignorance. He begins defining 'faculties' as the powers by which we operate.
distilled: Knowledge grasps what is; ignorance corresponds to what is not; opinion -- the intermediate faculty -- grasps what both is and is not.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This is the Divided Line argument in embryonic form, fully developed in Book VI (509d-511e) -- The tripartite epistemology (knowledge/opinion/ignorance) maps onto the tripartite ontology (being/becoming/non-being) -- 'Faculties' (dynameis) are distinguished by their sphere (object) and result (what they produce) -- The claim that knowledge and opinion are different faculties with different objects is controversial: it implies that the sensible world is not a proper object of knowledge -- This position is challenged in Aristotle's epistemology, which grounds knowledge in perception -- The 'something that is and is not' will be identified as the sensible world of becoming (genesis) versus the intelligible world of being (ousia)

narrative:
"Yes, I quite understand."

"Then let me tell you my view about them. I do not see them, and therefore the distinctions of fire, colour, and the like, which enable me to discern the differences of some things, do not apply to them. In speaking of a faculty I think only of its sphere and its result; and that which has the same sphere and the same result I call the same faculty, but that which has another sphere and another result I call different. Would that be your way of speaking?"
refashioned:
"Yes, I understand."

"Then here's my view of faculties. We can't see them directly, so the physical characteristics -- fire, color, shape -- that help us distinguish visible things don't apply. When I identify a faculty, I look at only two things: its object (what it's directed at) and its result (what it produces). If two faculties share the same object and the same result, they're the same faculty. If they differ in either object or result, they're different faculties. Would you define them the same way?"
elegant: Faculties are invisible and cannot be distinguished by physical characteristics. They are identified solely by two criteria: their object (sphere) and their result. Same object and result means the same faculty; different means different.
summary: Socrates defines how to distinguish faculties: since they are invisible, they cannot be differentiated by physical characteristics. Instead, a faculty is identified by its object (what it's directed at) and its result (what it produces). Different objects or results mean different faculties.
distilled: Faculties are distinguished by their objects and results -- not by physical properties -- so different objects mean different faculties.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- This methodological point is crucial: if knowledge and opinion have different objects, they are different faculties, which means they cannot share the same subject matter -- The criteria (sphere/object and result/product) provide a formal method for classifying mental powers -- This anticipates the Divided Line (Book VI), which maps different cognitive faculties to different segments of reality -- The invisible nature of faculties contrasts with the visible things that the sight-lovers prefer -- The argument has affinities with modern philosophy of mind's discussion of intentionality (aboutness) -- Aristotle adopts a similar criterion for distinguishing mental faculties in De Anima

narrative:
"And will you be so very good as to answer one more question? Would you say that knowledge is a faculty, or in what class would you place it?"

"Certainly knowledge is a faculty, and the mightiest of all faculties."

"And is opinion also a faculty?"

"Certainly," he said; "for opinion is that with which we are able to form an opinion."

"And yet you were acknowledging a little while ago that knowledge is not the same as opinion?"

"Why, yes," he said: "how can any reasonable being ever identify that which is infallible with that which errs?"

"An excellent answer, proving," I said, "that we are quite conscious of a distinction between them."

"Yes."

"Then knowledge and opinion having distinct powers have also distinct spheres or subject-matters?"

"That is certain."

"Being is the sphere or subject-matter of knowledge, and knowledge is to know the nature of being?"

"Yes."

"And opinion is to have an opinion?"

"Yes."

"And do we know what we opine? or is the subject-matter of opinion the same as the subject-matter of knowledge?"

"Nay," he replied, "that has been already disproven; if difference in faculty implies difference in the sphere or subject-matter, and if, as we were saying, opinion and knowledge are distinct faculties, then the sphere of knowledge and of opinion cannot be the same."

"Then if being is the subject-matter of knowledge, something else must be the subject-matter of opinion?"

"Yes, something else."

"Well then, is not-being the subject-matter of opinion? or, rather, how can there be an opinion at all about not-being? Reflect: when a man has an opinion, has he not an opinion about something? Can he have an opinion which is an opinion about nothing?"

"Impossible."

"He who has an opinion has an opinion about some one thing?"

"Yes."

"And not-being is not one thing but, properly speaking, nothing?"

"True."

"Of not-being, ignorance was assumed to be the necessary correlative; of being, knowledge?"

"True," he said.

"Then opinion is not concerned either with being or with not-being?"

"Not with either."

"And can therefore neither be ignorance nor knowledge?"

"That seems to be true."

"But is opinion to be sought without and beyond either of them, in a greater clearness than knowledge, or in a greater darkness than ignorance?"

"In neither."

"Then I suppose that opinion appears to you to be darker than knowledge, but lighter than ignorance?"
refashioned:
"One more question: is knowledge a faculty, or would you classify it differently?"

"Knowledge is certainly a faculty -- the most powerful of all."

"And opinion is also a faculty?"

"Yes -- opinion is the power by which we form opinions."

"But you agreed earlier that knowledge is not the same as opinion?"

"Of course -- how could any reasonable person equate something infallible with something that makes mistakes?"

"An excellent answer, which proves we clearly distinguish between them."

"Yes."

"Then since knowledge and opinion have distinct powers, they must have distinct objects?"

"Certainly."

"Being -- what truly exists -- is the object of knowledge?"

"Yes."

"And opinion is the power to form opinions. But is its object the same as knowledge's object?"

"No -- we've already shown that different faculties must have different objects. Since opinion and knowledge are distinct faculties, their objects cannot be the same."

"So if being is the object of knowledge, something else must be the object of opinion?"

"Yes."

"Is non-being the object of opinion? Actually, can there even be an opinion about non-being? Think about it: when someone has an opinion, it must be an opinion about something. Can you have an opinion about nothing?"

"Impossible."

"Someone with an opinion has an opinion about something that is one thing?"

"Yes."

"But non-being isn't one thing -- properly speaking, it's nothing at all."

"True."

"And we established that ignorance is the correlate of non-being, just as knowledge is the correlate of being?"

"True."

"Then opinion is concerned with neither being nor non-being?"

"Neither."

"So opinion is neither ignorance nor knowledge?"

"It seems so."

"Does opinion lie outside and beyond both of them -- clearer than knowledge, or darker than ignorance?"

"Neither."

"Then opinion strikes you as darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance?"
elegant: Knowledge is infallible and grasps being; ignorance corresponds to non-being. Opinion, being fallible but about something, cannot correspond to either pure being or pure non-being. It lies between them -- darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance -- corresponding to an intermediate realm between being and non-being.
summary: Socrates establishes the epistemological hierarchy: knowledge is infallible and grasps being (what fully exists); ignorance corresponds to non-being (what doesn't exist). Opinion, which is fallible but directed at something real, occupies the middle ground -- darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance -- and must correspond to something between being and non-being.
distilled: Knowledge grasps being infallibly; ignorance corresponds to non-being; opinion falls between them, darker than knowledge but lighter than ignorance.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The epistemological hierarchy (knowledge > opinion > ignorance) corresponds to the ontological hierarchy (being > becoming > non-being) -- 'Infallible' (anamarteton) versus 'errs' (hamartanon) is the key distinction between knowledge and opinion -- The argument that different faculties must have different objects is controversial and has been extensively debated -- This tripartite structure anticipates the Divided Line (Book VI, 509d-511e) where the visible and intelligible worlds are further subdivided -- The claim that opinion is 'about something' but not about being or non-being creates the logical space for the intermediate realm of becoming -- This is one of the most technically rigorous philosophical arguments in the Republic

narrative:
"Both; and in no small degree."

"And also to be within and between them?"

"Yes."

"Then you would infer that opinion is intermediate?"

"No question."

"But were we not saying before, that if anything appeared to be of a sort which is and is not at the same time, that sort of thing would appear also to lie in the interval between pure being and absolute not-being; and that the corresponding faculty is neither knowledge nor ignorance, but will be found in the interval between them?"
refashioned:
"Both -- significantly darker than knowledge and significantly lighter than ignorance."

"And it lies within and between them?"

"Yes."

"So opinion is intermediate between knowledge and ignorance."

"Without question."

"But didn't we say earlier that if anything appeared to both exist and not exist simultaneously, it would occupy the interval between pure being and absolute non-being? And the corresponding faculty would be neither knowledge nor ignorance, but something found in the interval between them?"
elegant: Opinion is confirmed as intermediate between knowledge and ignorance. Whatever both exists and does not exist simultaneously occupies the interval between being and non-being, grasped by the intermediate faculty of opinion.
summary: Opinion is definitively placed as intermediate between knowledge and ignorance. This confirms the earlier principle: whatever both exists and does not exist simultaneously occupies the space between pure being and absolute non-being, apprehended by the corresponding intermediate faculty.
distilled: Opinion is the intermediate faculty between knowledge and ignorance, grasping the intermediate reality that both is and is not.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The argument reaches its structural conclusion: three levels of cognition (knowledge, opinion, ignorance) map onto three levels of reality (being, becoming, non-being) -- 'Is and is not at the same time' characterizes the sensible world: a beautiful thing is also ugly in some respect, a large thing is also small relative to something else -- This framework is the philosophical foundation of the Divided Line and the Cave allegory -- The 'interval' (metaxy) between being and non-being is the realm of becoming (genesis), which is the sensible/physical world -- Plato's claim that the physical world is not fully real but not nothing either is one of the most influential and debated ideas in Western philosophy

narrative:
"True."

"And in that interval there has now been discovered something which we call opinion?"

"There has."

"Then what remains to be discovered is the object which partakes equally of the nature of being and not-being, and cannot rightly be termed either, pure and simple; this unknown term, when discovered, we may truly call the subject of opinion, and assign each to its proper faculty, -- the extremes to the faculties of the extremes and the mean to the faculty of the mean."
refashioned:
"And in that interval we've now identified something we call opinion."

"We have."

"What remains is to discover the object that partakes equally of being and non-being -- that cannot properly be called either one, purely and simply. Once we identify this object, we can rightfully call it the subject of opinion and assign each level of reality to its corresponding faculty: the extremes (being and non-being) to their respective faculties (knowledge and ignorance), and the intermediate to its faculty (opinion)."
elegant: With opinion identified as the intermediate faculty, what remains is to find its object: something that partakes equally of being and non-being. This will complete the mapping of faculties to realities -- knowledge to being, ignorance to non-being, opinion to the intermediate.
summary: Having established opinion as the intermediate faculty, Socrates seeks its corresponding object: something that partakes equally of being and non-being. This will complete the systematic mapping: knowledge corresponds to being, ignorance to non-being, and opinion to the intermediate realm.
distilled: The object of opinion is whatever partakes equally of being and non-being, completing the faculty-reality mapping.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon -- The systematic mapping (faculty to object) creates a complete epistemological-ontological framework -- 'Partakes equally of being and not-being' describes the sensible world: physical things come into being and pass away, are beautiful and ugly, large and small -- The 'extremes and the mean' language reflects mathematical proportionality, connecting to the geometric structure of the Divided Line -- This passage sets up the final argument of Book V (5.86-5.88), which identifies the many beautiful things of the sensible world as the proper objects of opinion -- The complete framework: Forms (being) -> Knowledge; Sensible things (becoming) -> Opinion; Nothing (non-being) -> Ignorance

narrative:
"True."

"This being premised, I would ask the gentleman who is of opinion that there is no absolute or unchangeable idea of beauty -- in whose opinion the beautiful is the manifold -- he, I say, your lover of beautiful sights, who cannot bear to be told that the beautiful is one, and the just is one, or that anything is one -- to him I would appeal, saying, Will you be so very kind, sir, as to tell us whether, of all these beautiful things, there is one which will not be found ugly; or of the just, which will not be found unjust; or of the holy, which will not also be unholy?"
refashioned:
"With this established, let me address the person who believes there is no absolute or unchanging Form of Beauty -- who holds that beauty is simply the manifold of beautiful things. This lover of beautiful sights, who cannot tolerate being told that Beauty is one, or that Justice is one, or that anything is one -- to this person I would say:

'Would you be so kind as to tell us: among all these beautiful things you love, is there one that won't also be found ugly from some perspective? Among all things you call just, is there one that won't also appear unjust? Among all things holy, is there one that won't also seem unholy?'"
elegant: Socrates challenges the sight-lover who denies the Forms: among all the beautiful things in the sensible world, can you find even one that won't also appear ugly? Among the just, one that won't seem unjust? The sensible world's particulars always exhibit contradictory properties.
summary: Socrates challenges those who deny the existence of absolute Forms: can any particular beautiful thing never appear ugly? Can anything just never seem unjust? The sensible world's objects always exhibit contradictory properties, proving they are not absolute being.
